# Raw runtime-vulnerability catalog (P1 research, 269 entries)

Unranked research output across 18 slices. Ranking + adversarial review (P3) produces the curated Top 51 + extended list separately. Each entry is *runtime-discoverable* (found by interacting with a running app).

## Java / Spring  (15)

- 🔴 **Spring Boot Actuator Endpoints Exposed** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the running app for /actuator and its children (/actuator/env, /actuator/health, /actuator/heapdump, /actuator/mappings, /actuator/beans, /actuator/loggers, /actuator/trace, /actuator/httptrace). Enumerate both /actuator/* and legacy /management/* paths. Probe each without auth tokens.
  - signal: HTTP 200 with JSON body containing keys like 'propertySources', 'contexts', 'beans', 'mappings', or a binary response (heapdump) from /actuator/heapdump. A 404 on /actuator but 200 on /actuator/health (partial exposure) is also flagged.
- 🟠 **Actuator /env Leaks Credentials via Partial Masking Bypass** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: GET /actuator/env without credentials. Then probe /actuator/env/{property-name} for individual entries (e.g., /actuator/env/spring.datasource.password). Compare key list against a dictionary of known secret property names.
  - signal: Response body from /actuator/env contains property keys matching regex (password|secret|key|token|credential|api[-_]?key) even if values show '******'. A single-key probe /actuator/env/spring.datasource.password returning 200 with an unmasked value is a direct hit.
- 🟠 **Broken Object-Level Authorization (IDOR) via Missing @PreAuthorize** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Auth as user A, collect resource IDs from their normal responses. Auth as user B, substitute user A's IDs into identical requests. If user B receives user A's data, IDOR is confirmed. Also fuzz sequential/UUIDs in path and query parameters.
  - signal: User B's request for /api/orders/{userA_id} returns HTTP 200 with user A's data rather than 403/404. Auth-differential comparison: same endpoint, same ID, different session tokens produces matching response bodies.
- 🟠 **Mass Assignment via Spring Data / @RequestBody Binding** — _A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Intercept a normal POST/PUT request body. Inject extra fields by appending 'role':'admin', 'isAdmin':true, 'enabled':true, 'balance':99999 to the JSON payload. Submit and observe whether the server reflects the injected values back in subsequent GET responses or login behavior changes.
  - signal: A follow-up GET for the same resource returns the injected field value (e.g., 'role':'admin'). HTTP 200 on the mutating request with the injected field reflected in the response body. Elevation detected via auth-differential: re-auth after injection shows elevated permissions.
- 🟡 **CSRF Protection Disabled in Spring Security Config** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Authenticate via browser session. Check whether state-changing endpoints (POST /api/transfer, POST /api/settings) succeed without a CSRF token header or parameter. Observe Set-Cookie response header for session cookies lacking SameSite=Strict/Lax.
  - signal: POST to a state-changing endpoint returns HTTP 200/201 when no X-XSRF-TOKEN or _csrf parameter is included, and the session cookie is present. Also: absence of XSRF-TOKEN cookie in Set-Cookie headers that would normally accompany Angular/Axios auto-CSRF.
- 🔴 **H2 Database Console Exposed in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Probe /h2-console and /h2-console/login.jsp. Also check /console (alternate path). Attempt access without authentication.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response from /h2-console with HTML containing 'H2 Console' title or a login form with 'JDBC URL' and 'User Name' fields. Any successful connection to the console without prior app authentication is a critical finding.
- 🟡 **Open Redirect via Spring Security Login Redirect Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Append ?redirect_to=https://evil.com, ?spring-security-redirect=https://evil.com, or ?continue=//evil.com to the login URL. Authenticate successfully and observe the Location header in the 302 response.
  - signal: HTTP 302 Location header after successful login points to an external or non-origin URL (e.g., https://evil.com or //evil.com). Any redirect leaving the application's origin after auth is flagged.
- 🔴 **JWT 'none' Algorithm Accepted by Spring Security** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Capture a valid JWT from a normal login response. Modify the header alg to 'none' and strip the signature. Also try alg:'HS256' with the public key as the HMAC secret. Replay the modified token on protected endpoints.
  - signal: HTTP 200 on a protected endpoint when the tampered token (alg:none, no signature) is sent in the Authorization header. Any successful authenticated response with a forged/unsigned JWT is a critical hit.
- 🟠 **JPQL / HQL Injection via Spring Data @Query** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Inject JPQL metacharacters into search/filter form fields and query parameters: single quote ('), ' OR '1'='1, ' AND 1=2--, LIKE %'. Observe whether the response changes (returns extra records), throws a 500 with a Hibernate exception, or changes record count.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body containing 'org.hibernate.', 'QuerySyntaxException', 'antlr', or 'unexpected token' when a single quote or JPQL keyword is injected. Alternatively, injecting ' OR '1'='1 causes the endpoint to return all records instead of filtered results.
- 🟠 **Overly Permissive CORS Configuration with allowCredentials** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send requests with arbitrary Origin headers (Origin: https://evil.com) to API endpoints. Inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header — if it reflects back the attacker origin AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is also present, the configuration is exploitable.
  - signal: Response contains both 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com' (reflected attacker origin) and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true'. Any combination of a non-null, non-same-origin ACAO with ACAC:true is flagged.
- 🟡 **Spring Security / Whitelabel Error Page Leaks Stack Traces** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Trigger errors by sending malformed JSON bodies, unexpected content types (Content-Type: text/xml instead of application/json), invalid path parameters (e.g., /api/users/notanumber for a Long param), and oversized payloads. Observe the response body for stack traces.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body containing 'java.lang.', 'org.springframework.', 'at com.', or a JSON object with 'trace' key containing multiline stack frame strings. Also: HTML response with 'Whitelabel Error Page' and an exception message.
- 🟠 **Actuator /loggers Endpoint Allows Log Level Manipulation** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: GET /actuator/loggers to confirm access. Then POST to /actuator/loggers/org.springframework.security with body {"configuredLevel":"TRACE"}. Attempt to read /actuator/logfile afterward. Also attempt POST to /actuator/loggers/ROOT to set all logging to DEBUG.
  - signal: HTTP 200 on POST to /actuator/loggers/{name} without authentication. Subsequent /actuator/logfile or /actuator/httptrace responses containing authentication credentials, tokens, or sensitive request parameters in log lines.
- 🔴 **Vertical Privilege Escalation via Missing Role Checks on Admin Endpoints** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: With a low-privilege authenticated session, enumerate admin-looking paths discovered from error messages, JS source, or /actuator/mappings. Try path case variations, trailing slashes, semicolon injection, and double slashes. Compare HTTP response codes and body content between admin and non-admin sessions.
  - signal: Low-privilege session receives HTTP 200 with admin-level data on a path variant (e.g., /API/Admin/users or /api/admin/users/) that the canonical path would have returned 403 for. Auth-differential: same path variant returns different body for admin vs regular user.
- 🟡 **Session Fixation — Session ID Not Rotated After Authentication** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: Record the session cookie (JSESSIONID) value from a pre-authentication request. Authenticate successfully. Compare the session cookie value in the post-authentication response — if it is identical to the pre-auth value, session fixation protection is absent.
  - signal: JSESSIONID (or equivalent Spring Session cookie) value is identical before and after a successful POST to /login. The Set-Cookie header in the login success response is absent (no new cookie issued) or contains the same token value.
- 🔴 **Spring Data REST Auto-Exports All Repositories as Unauthenticated HATEOAS Endpoints** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: GET the HATEOAS discovery root (/ or /api) to enumerate all auto-exported collection endpoints from _links. Probe each collection without authentication. Attempt GET (list), POST (create), PATCH (modify), DELETE on each endpoint as an unauthenticated user and as a low-privilege user.
  - signal: Unauthenticated GET to /api returns JSON with _links listing multiple entity collections. Fetching any collection link without a session returns HTTP 200 with entity data. A POST or PATCH returning 201/200 without authentication or for a resource the user should not own is a direct finding.

## Node.js / Express  (15)

- ⚪ **Express X-Powered-By Header Technology Disclosure** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  low/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Crawl any endpoint and inspect response headers. Record the presence and value of `X-Powered-By`, `Server`, and `Via` headers across all observed responses to build a technology fingerprint.
  - signal: Response header `X-Powered-By: Express` present on one or more URLs. Also watch for `Server: node` variants. Signal fires on any 2xx, 3xx, or 4xx response.
- 🟠 **CORS Wildcard + Credentialed Request Misconfiguration** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send OPTIONS preflight and GET/POST requests with an `Origin: https://attacker.example.com` header and a valid session cookie. Compare the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` response headers.
  - signal: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` echoes back the injected attacker origin AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` appears in the same response. Both headers present together on a credentialed request is the exploitable combination.
- 🟡 **serve-static / express.static Directory Listing Enabled** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: Crawl all static path prefixes (typically `/static`, `/public`, `/assets`, `/dist`). Send GET requests to directory-level URLs (trailing slash). Observe whether the response contains an HTML `<ul>` or `<table>` of file names.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response to a directory URL with `Content-Type: text/html` and body containing repeated `<a href=` file links or a visible folder listing pattern (e.g., `Index of /`).
- 🔴 **JSON Body Prototype Pollution via body-parser** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Submit POST/PUT/PATCH requests to every JSON-accepting endpoint with a body containing `{"__proto__": {"isAdmin": true}}` and `{"constructor": {"prototype": {"polluted": "yes"}}}`. Then probe a subsequent GET endpoint to check if the injected key appears in server behavior (e.g., elevated privilege, changed response field).
  - signal: Subsequent request to an unpolluted endpoint exhibits changed behavior (different role, 200 instead of 403, new field in response JSON) after the prototype-pollution payload was submitted. Alternatively, a 500 error with a Node.js stack trace containing `Cannot set property` on a null prototype chain.
- 🟠 **Mass Assignment via Unsanitized req.body** — _A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit registration or profile-update forms with extra JSON fields beyond what the UI exposes (`isAdmin`, `role`, `accountBalance`, `verified`). Compare the resulting resource (via a subsequent GET) to see if the injected field was persisted.
  - signal: A subsequent authenticated GET on the resource (user profile, product) returns the injected field value unchanged, or behavior changes (elevated access) consistent with the field being written. Auth-differential: User A submits `{"role":"admin"}`, then compare role-restricted endpoint responses between User A and a normal User B.
- 🟡 **Missing or Weak Content-Security-Policy (helmet not applied)** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/passive
  - discovery: Inspect response headers from the application root and a sample of authenticated endpoints. Check for `Content-Security-Policy` and `X-Content-Type-Options` and `X-Frame-Options` headers.
  - signal: `Content-Security-Policy` header absent from HTML responses. Bonus signals: also missing `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, or `Strict-Transport-Security`.
- 🟠 **express-session Insecure Cookie Defaults (no Secure/HttpOnly/SameSite)** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/common/passive
  - discovery: Authenticate and inspect the `Set-Cookie` header for the session cookie. Check for `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, and `SameSite` attributes. Also compare the cookie name against known default values (`connect.sid`).
  - signal: `Set-Cookie` header on the login response contains `connect.sid=...` without `Secure` and/or without `SameSite`. Alternatively, the cookie name is the unchanged default `connect.sid`, which fingerprints the exact middleware.
- 🔴 **Path Traversal Through Static File Middleware** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Fuzz static-file endpoint URL path segments with traversal sequences: `../`, `..%2F`, `..%5C`, `%2e%2e%2f`, `....//`. Target known static prefixes and any URL parameter that maps to a file path. Observe 200 responses and content-type changes.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response to a traversal URL with `Content-Type: text/plain` or `application/octet-stream` and body matching file content of a known server-side file (e.g., `root:` in `/etc/passwd`, or `package.json` contents). A 403 on normal paths but 200 on encoded traversal is also a signal.
- 🟡 **Open Redirect in res.redirect() with User-Supplied URL** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Enumerate all redirect-triggering endpoints (login, logout, OAuth callbacks). Inject `?next=https://attacker.example.com`, `?redirect_uri=//evil.com`, and URL-encoded variants. Follow or inspect the `Location` header of 3xx responses.
  - signal: HTTP 301/302/303 response with `Location` header containing the injected attacker domain verbatim (e.g., `https://attacker.example.com`). Scanner confirms the reflected URL is not a same-site relative path.
- 🔴 **JWT Algorithm Confusion / 'alg:none' Acceptance** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Obtain a valid JWT from the login endpoint. Craft a new token with the header `{"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}`, sign with an empty string, and send it in the `Authorization` header. Also try switching from RS256 to HS256 signed with the app's public key as the HMAC secret.
  - signal: A 200 response (instead of 401/403) when sending a forged `alg:none` token or an HS256 token signed with the known public key. Auth-differential: the forged token grants the same access level as a legitimately issued token.
- 🔴 **NoSQL Injection via MongoDB Operator Injection** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: For login and search endpoints, replace string fields with JSON operator objects: send `{"username": {"$gt": ""}, "password": {"$gt": ""}}` as the request body. Also test query-string form: `?filter[$ne]=x`. Observe authentication success or unexpected data returned.
  - signal: Login returns a valid session/token (HTTP 200 with auth cookie) when credentials are replaced with `{"$gt": ""}`, or a search endpoint returns more records than expected with operator-injected parameters. A 500 with a MongoDB error message is also a positive signal revealing the injection point.
- 🟠 **Debug / Admin Routes Exposed Without Authentication** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Enumerate a wordlist of common Express debug/admin path fragments (`/metrics`, `/health`, `/healthz`, `/__status`, `/admin`, `/graphql`, `/api-docs`, `/swagger.json`, `/openapi.json`, `/env`) as unauthenticated requests. Record 200 responses and analyze response bodies.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response to an unauthenticated request to a sensitive path containing Prometheus metric lines (`# HELP`, `# TYPE`), JSON with `process.env`, an OpenAPI schema object (`{"openapi":"3.`), or a GraphQL introspection result (`__schema`).
- 🟠 **Insecure Direct Object Reference via Sequential / Guessable IDs** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Auth-differential: Authenticate as User A and record their resource ID (e.g., `/api/users/1042`). Authenticate as User B and attempt to GET/PUT/DELETE User A's resource ID using User B's session token. Compare HTTP status codes and response body contents.
  - signal: User B's authenticated request to User A's resource returns HTTP 200 with User A's data (not a 403). Alternatively, incrementing the numeric ID in a URL returns different users' data with the same session. Response body differs between User A and User B's own resource but is accessible to both.
- 🟠 **Rate Limiting Absent or Bypassable on Sensitive Endpoints** — _A04:2021 Insecure Design_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send 50-200 rapid POST requests to `/login`, `/forgot-password`, and `/verify-otp` endpoints. Also send the same burst with varying `X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.x` headers to test key-generator bypass. Measure response status codes and time-to-first-429.
  - signal: No HTTP 429 after 50+ rapid identical login attempts, or: a 429 is triggered on the real IP but disappears when `X-Forwarded-For` is rotated. Timing uniformity (no delay increase) across 100 requests also confirms absent rate limiting.
- 🔴 **Server-Side Request Forgery via User-Controlled URL in axios / got** — _A10:2021 Server-Side Request Forgery_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Identify endpoints that fetch external content (webhook testers, URL preview/screenshot features, import-from-URL flows). Inject internal targets: `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/`, `http://localhost:6379/`, `http://127.0.0.1:8080/admin`, and a known collaborator URL to confirm out-of-band requests.
  - signal: Response body contains AWS/GCP/Azure cloud metadata content (e.g., `ami-id`, `instance-id`, `iam/security-credentials`). Alternatively, a collaborator server receives an inbound HTTP request from the app's IP after the probe. Or a 200 response with Redis inline protocol text (`+OK`, `-ERR`).

## Next.js  (15)

- 🟠 **Next.js API Route Missing HTTP Method Guard** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: For each discovered API endpoint (crawled from HTML, JS bundles, or sitemap), replay the request with all HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS). Compare status codes and response bodies across verbs. A 200 or 201 on a destructive verb that should return 405 is a confirmed finding.
  - signal: HTTP 200/201/204 on DELETE, PUT, or PATCH to an endpoint where only GET/POST was observed in normal app traffic; response body indicates state change (e.g., `{"deleted":true}`, `{"updated":true}`).
- 🔴 **Next.js API Route Unauthenticated Access (Missing Session Check)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Replay every API request observed during an authenticated crawl session, but strip the session cookie / Authorization header. Compare response: if the unauthenticated replay returns the same 200 with data as the authenticated version, the route is unprotected. Enumerate additional routes by fuzzing common Next.js patterns (`/api/user`, `/api/me`, `/api/admin/users`, `/api/dashboard`).
  - signal: HTTP 200 with non-empty JSON body on an API route when the Authorization / Cookie header is absent or replaced with an invalid token; auth-diff between user-A session and no-session returns identical payload.
- 🔴 **Next.js Middleware Auth Bypass via `x-middleware-subrequest` Header (CVE-2025-29927)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: For every route that redirects unauthenticated requests (observed as 302/307 during an anonymous crawl), replay the request adding the header `x-middleware-subrequest: middleware` (or `pages/_middleware`). If the response changes from a redirect to a 200 with protected content, the middleware is bypassed.
  - signal: Route previously returning HTTP 302/307 redirect to `/login` now returns HTTP 200 with protected page content when `x-middleware-subrequest: middleware` header is present; `x-powered-by: Next.js` header confirms Next.js stack.
- 🟠 **Next.js Server Action IDOR / Missing Ownership Check** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Intercept form submissions that trigger server actions (identified by `Next-Action` request header or form `action` attribute pointing to the same URL with a POST). Replay the same POST as a second authenticated user (auth-diff), or enumerate adjacent integer/UUID IDs in the action payload. Compare responses for data leakage or modification success.
  - signal: POST to page URL with `Next-Action: <action-id>` header; substituting a resource ID belonging to another user returns HTTP 200 with that user's data or a success confirmation instead of a 403/404.
- 🟠 **Next.js RSC Sensitive Props Leaked to Client Bundle** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/occasional/passive
  - discovery: Crawl pages and inspect inline `<script id='__NEXT_DATA__'>` JSON and `__RSC_MANIFEST__` blocks in the HTML source. Also fetch `/_next/static/chunks/*.js` and search for high-entropy strings, email patterns, internal URLs, or secret-shaped tokens. No authentication needed for publicly accessible pages.
  - signal: Regex match for API key patterns (e.g., `sk-`, `AKIA`, `ghp_`), internal hostnames, or PII inside `__NEXT_DATA__` JSON blob or `_next/static` JS chunks; or RSC flight payload (`text/x-component`) containing credential-shaped strings.
- 🟡 **Next.js Open Redirect in API Route or `next.config.js` Redirects** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Enumerate query parameters named `redirect`, `return`, `returnUrl`, `next`, `url`, `continue`, `goto` across all API routes and pages. Inject `https://evil.com` and observe the Location header in the response. Also fuzz the `destination` parameter on any observed `/api/auth/*` (e.g., NextAuth callback URLs).
  - signal: HTTP 302/307/308 response with `Location: https://evil.com` (or any external domain not in the app's allowlist) after injecting an external URL into a redirect-shaped query parameter.
- 🔴 **Next.js SSR/API Route SSRF via User-Controlled URL** — _A10:2021 Server-Side Request Forgery_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Identify parameters that appear to control upstream URLs (e.g., `?url=`, `?src=`, `?endpoint=`, `?host=`, `?proxy=`). Inject `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (AWS IMDSv1), `http://localhost:6379` (Redis), and `http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/` (GCP). Measure response time difference vs. a known-unreachable host for blind SSRF. Observe response body for internal data.
  - signal: Response body contains AWS instance metadata structure, Redis RESP protocol bytes, or internal JSON not matching public API contracts; OR >2 s response time delta compared to baseline when injecting non-routable IPs (blind SSRF timing signal).
- 🟠 **Next.js API Route Overly Permissive CORS** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send an OPTIONS preflight and a credentialed cross-origin GET/POST to each API route with `Origin: https://evil.example.com`. Inspect response headers. If `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` echoes back the injected origin AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` is present, the route is exploitable for cross-origin data theft.
  - signal: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com` mirrors back the injected Origin header AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` is set in the same response.
- 🔴 **Next.js Path Traversal in Custom Static File / Image Serving Routes** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Enumerate API routes or Next.js Image Optimization proxy endpoints that accept a filename or path query parameter. Inject `../../../../etc/passwd`, `..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd`, and `....//....//etc/passwd`. Also try `../../../.env` and `../../../next.config.js`. Compare response content to baseline.
  - signal: Response body contains Unix `/etc/passwd` format lines, `.env` `KEY=VALUE` pairs, or `next.config.js` JavaScript source; HTTP 200 with `Content-Type: text/plain` or `application/octet-stream` for a path-parameter-driven endpoint.
- 🔴 **Next.js `NEXT_PUBLIC_` Secret Leakage in Client Bundle** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/common/passive
  - discovery: Fetch all `/_next/static/chunks/*.js` files discovered from the app's HTML. Apply regex patterns for known secret formats (Stripe `sk_live_`, AWS `AKIA`, SendGrid `SG.`, etc.) and also search for the literal string `NEXT_PUBLIC_`. No authentication needed.
  - signal: String match for `NEXT_PUBLIC_` followed by a secret-shaped value (high-entropy string, API key prefix) inside `/_next/static/chunks/` JavaScript files; or direct match for known secret prefixes (`sk_live_`, `AKIA`, `SG.`) in bundle source.
- 🟡 **Next.js Error Overlay / Stack Trace Exposure in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: Trigger server errors by sending malformed JSON bodies, invalid query parameter types, or non-existent route parameters to SSR pages and API routes. Observe the HTML response body for stack trace patterns (`Error:`, `at Object.<anonymous>`, file system paths `/home/`, `/app/`, `node_modules/`). Also check the `x-nextjs-cache` and `x-powered-by` headers for version disclosure.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body contains a JavaScript stack trace string with absolute file paths (e.g., `/app/pages/api/`, `/home/node/`) or the Next.js development error overlay HTML (`__NEXT_ERROR__`, `_next/static/development/`); or `x-powered-by: Next.js` combined with version number in error body.
- 🟠 **Next.js Server Action Cross-Site Request Forgery** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Identify server action endpoints (POST to page URL with `Next-Action` header). Replay the POST from a different origin by setting `Origin: https://evil.example.com` and omitting the Referer header. If the action executes successfully (HTTP 200 with mutation result rather than 403), CSRF protection is absent or bypassed.
  - signal: Server action POST with `Origin: https://evil.example.com` returns HTTP 200 with action result JSON (not a CSRF rejection); response body matches expected mutation output (e.g., `{"status":"ok"}`); `Next-Action` header is accepted cross-origin.
- 🟠 **Next.js / Node.js Prototype Pollution via Query String Parsing** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Inject prototype-polluting payloads into query strings and JSON request bodies: `?__proto__[isAdmin]=true`, `?constructor[prototype][role]=admin`, and `{"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true}}` in POST bodies. Then issue a follow-up request that exercises the affected auth check. Observe if the response indicates elevated privilege or different behavior.
  - signal: Follow-up request after prototype-pollution probe returns a response body or HTTP status (200 vs 403) consistent with elevated privilege; or the app returns a 500 error with a stack trace referencing `Object.prototype` when the pollution payload causes a type error downstream.
- 🟠 **Next.js i18n Locale Prefix Middleware Auth Bypass** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: For every path that redirects unauthenticated requests to login (observed as 302 during anonymous crawl), prefix it with each configured locale slug (`/en/`, `/fr/`, `/de/`) and any arbitrary locale slug. Compare responses. If the locale-prefixed path returns 200 with protected content instead of a redirect, middleware matching is incomplete.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response with protected page HTML content at `/en/<protected-path>` where `/protected-path>` returns HTTP 302; locale-prefixed URL bypasses session check; `set-cookie` header is absent on the 200 response confirming no auth was enforced.
- 🟠 **Next.js `rewrites()` Exposing Internal / Admin API Routes** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Crawl the app and enumerate all observed API route patterns. Fuzz common admin/internal path aliases (`/api/internal/*`, `/api/admin/*`, `/_internal/*`, `/api/v1/admin/*`). Also probe `/_next/` subpaths beyond static assets. Look for mismatched access control between the canonical route and any rewrite aliases discovered.
  - signal: A fuzzed path returns HTTP 200 with admin-shaped JSON (user lists, config data, internal metrics) where the canonical protected route would return 401/403; or the response body is identical to a known admin endpoint but accessible without an admin session cookie.

## React SPA  (15)

- 🔴 **REACT_APP_* / VITE_ Secrets Baked Into JS Bundle** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Crawl the app and collect all .js chunk URLs from the HTML. Fetch each chunk and scan the response body for high-entropy strings, known key prefixes (sk_, AIza, AKIA, ghp_, etc.), and well-known variable name patterns like REACT_APP_ or VITE_. No authentication required.
  - signal: Regex match of known secret prefixes (sk_live_, AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}, AIzaSy, ghp_) or high-entropy Base64/hex strings of length 20+ inside a text/javascript response body fetched from a public chunk URL.
- 🟠 **Client-Side-Only Authorization (Hidden Route / Conditional Render Bypass)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Parse the JS bundle for React Router route definitions (Route, createBrowserRouter path values) and collect every path string. Attempt direct navigation to each path as an unauthenticated or low-privilege user. Observe whether the app renders protected content or whether the API calls succeed when made directly.
  - signal: A route path extracted from the bundle (e.g. /admin, /billing, /internal) returns HTTP 200 with meaningful HTML/JSON content when accessed directly by a non-admin session, rather than redirecting (302) or returning 401/403.
- 🟠 **Redux / Zustand Store Exposed in Window or DevTools** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: After logging in, inject a short script via browser console (or via the scanner's injected content script) to read window.__REDUX_DEVTOOLS_EXTENSION__ presence, window.__STORE__, or window.__ZUSTAND__. Serialize and inspect the state tree for sensitive keys (token, accessToken, ssn, dob, email).
  - signal: window.__REDUX_DEVTOOLS_EXTENSION__ is truthy in a production build, OR window[storeKey] resolves to an object with a getState() method that returns fields matching secret/token/credential patterns.
- 🟠 **Overly Permissive CORS on the Backing REST/GraphQL API** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send a preflight OPTIONS (and then a credentialed GET/POST) to each discovered API endpoint with an arbitrary Origin: https://evil.example.com header. Inspect the response headers for the reflected or wildcard ACAO value and whether ACAC: true is present simultaneously.
  - signal: Response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com (reflected) together with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, or ACAO: * with credentials mode allowing reads of sensitive data.
- 🟠 **JWT / Bearer Token Stored in localStorage (XSS-Accessible)** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: After authenticating, read localStorage and sessionStorage keys via injected script. Match key names containing 'token', 'jwt', 'auth', 'access', 'bearer'. Decode the Base64url payload to confirm it is a JWT. Also check for tokens stored in non-HttpOnly cookies.
  - signal: localStorage.getItem(key) or document.cookie returns a value matching the JWT three-part Base64url pattern (eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]+), confirmed by a decodable header with alg field.
- 🟠 **dangerouslySetInnerHTML / innerHTML Sink Leading to Stored/Reflected XSS** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Fill text inputs, URL parameters, and profile fields with an XSS canary payload (e.g. <img src=x onerror=window.__xss=1>). Submit and then navigate to any page that would render that content. Check whether window.__xss is set, or intercept network requests for an out-of-band callback.
  - signal: Injected canary string appears unescaped in a subsequent page's DOM (innerHTML contains literal <script> or event handler attributes), OR the onerror/onload callback fires as confirmed by window property set or out-of-band DNS/HTTP ping.
- 🟡 **Open Redirect via SPA Router ?redirect= / returnTo= Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Identify login or callback URLs by crawling. Append ?redirect=https://evil.example.com (and variants: //evil.example.com, /\evil.example.com, %2F%2Fevil.example.com). Trigger the login flow and observe the final navigation target after authentication.
  - signal: After the authentication flow completes, the browser location ends up at a host other than the app's own origin, or the network log shows a 302 Location header pointing to the injected external URL.
- 🔴 **Firebase / Supabase / BaaS Config Object Exposed in Bundle** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Fetch all JS chunk URLs. Search response bodies for known BaaS config object shapes: firebaseConfig = { apiKey:, createClient( with url and anonKey, or Amplify.configure({ with aws_user_pools_id. Extract the key values and attempt unauthenticated Firestore/REST API reads against the project.
  - signal: JS chunk body contains the literal string firebaseConfig or SUPABASE_ANON_KEY alongside a project ID, followed by a successful unauthenticated GET to https://<project>.firebaseio.com/.json or https://<project>.supabase.co/rest/v1/<table> returning non-empty data.
- 🟠 **IDOR via Client-Controlled Object IDs in API Calls** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Log in as user A, capture API requests containing numeric or UUID resource identifiers (e.g. GET /api/orders/1234). Log in as user B and replay those requests substituting user A's IDs. Compare the responses: if user B receives user A's data, IDOR is confirmed.
  - signal: Auth-diff: an authenticated request made with user B's session token to a URL containing user A's resource ID returns HTTP 200 with a non-empty body matching user A's data schema, instead of 403 or an empty/error response.
- 🟡 **Production Source Maps Publicly Accessible** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Fetch each discovered .js chunk URL and check the final comment for //# sourceMappingURL= pointing to a .map file. Request that .map URL directly. If it returns HTTP 200 with a JSON body containing a sources array, source reconstruction is possible.
  - signal: A .js.map URL returns HTTP 200 Content-Type application/json with a top-level sources array containing recognizable file paths (src/components/, src/api/) and a non-empty sourcesContent array.
- 🟡 **GraphQL Introspection Enabled in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Identify the GraphQL endpoint from network traffic or bundle strings (typically /graphql, /api/graphql). Send a POST with the standard introspection query { __schema { types { name } } }. Parse the response for a full type list.
  - signal: POST to the GraphQL endpoint with Content-Type application/json body {"query":"{__schema{types{name}}}"} returns HTTP 200 with a data.__schema.types array containing more than 10 entries including built-in scalar types.
- 🟡 **Sensitive Data Leaked in URL Query Parameters / Hash Fragment** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  medium/common/passive
  - discovery: Crawl the app and capture all navigation URLs (including redirects). Inspect query strings and hash fragments for patterns matching tokens (JWT, UUID, long hex), email addresses, phone numbers, or other PII. Also observe OAuth callback URLs for token fragments.
  - signal: A navigated-to URL contains a query parameter or fragment matching JWT pattern, high-entropy token (32+ hex chars or Base64url), email address regex, or numeric ID combined with PII field name keys (ssn=, dob=, phone=).
- 🟠 **Insecure postMessage Origin Validation (Wildcard or Missing)** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Load the app in an iframe (or open as a child window) from a controlled test page. Dispatch postMessage events with crafted payloads (fake auth tokens, action commands) and observe whether the app processes them. Also listen for outgoing postMessage calls with sensitive data and no origin restriction.
  - signal: A postMessage listener in the app's JS bundle (found via source map or bundle analysis) lacks an event.origin check, OR the scanner's controlled frame receives a postMessage containing token/auth/credential fields when the parent sends a trigger message, OR the app mutates its state in response to an untrusted-origin message.
- 🟠 **Client-Side Feature Flag / Role Check Bypass** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Identify feature flag keys or role field names from the bundle or network responses. Modify the stored flag value in localStorage, sessionStorage, or the in-memory store via injected script (e.g. localStorage.setItem('flags', JSON.stringify({adminMode: true}))). Reload and observe whether protected UI and the backing API calls become accessible.
  - signal: After overwriting a flag or role value in client storage, the app renders previously hidden UI elements (confirmed by DOM diff) AND the corresponding API calls succeed with HTTP 200, confirming the server also trusts the client-supplied role.
- 🟡 **Third-Party Script Loaded Without SRI / From Uncontrolled CDN** — _A06:2021 Vulnerable and Outdated Components_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Crawl the app's HTML responses and collect all <script src=> tags pointing to external origins. For each, check whether the integrity= attribute is present and non-empty. Also inspect dynamically injected scripts by observing the DOM after the page fully loads.
  - signal: A <script src=https://[third-party-domain]/...> tag is present in the live page DOM without an integrity= attribute containing a sha256- or sha384- hash, AND the domain is not the app's own origin or a first-party CDN path.

## Angular / Vue  (15)

- 🔴 **Angular Client-Side Template Injection (CSTI) via Expression Sandbox Escape** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Submit payloads like {{7*7}}, {{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}} or <img [src]='x' (error)='constructor.constructor("alert(1)")()'> into every text input, URL parameter, and search field. Observe the rendered DOM for evaluated arithmetic (49 appearing instead of {{7*7}}) or JS execution.
  - signal: Response body or live DOM contains '49' when input '{{7*7}}' was submitted; or JavaScript alert fires; or Angular error overlay references 'unsafe value used in a resource URL context' paired with reflected input.
- 🟠 **Angular Client-Side Route Guard Bypass** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the SPA to discover all route paths from the JS bundle (look for RouterModule.forRoot or loadChildren chunks). Issue direct HTTP requests to the backing API endpoints using an unauthenticated or low-privilege session token. Compare HTTP response codes and data payloads between an admin session and the unauthenticated direct call.
  - signal: Direct API call (e.g., GET /api/admin/users) returns HTTP 200 with full payload while the same call with the unauthenticated browser session returns HTTP 302 to /login — confirming guard bypass. Alternatively, the guarded route renders in the browser with no valid JWT present in localStorage/sessionStorage.
- 🟠 **Vue v-html Directive XSS** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Identify input fields whose values appear rendered as rich text or formatted content in the UI (e.g., comment bodies, profile bios, message previews). Submit <img src=x onerror=alert(1)> and <script>alert(1)</script> payloads. Inspect the rendered DOM via DevTools to see if HTML is injected as a node rather than escaped text.
  - signal: Submitted payload <img src=x onerror=alert(1)> appears in the DOM as an actual <img> element (not &lt;img&gt;) and triggers the onerror handler; or DevTools Elements panel shows raw HTML nodes rather than escaped text content inside a Vue-controlled element.
- 🟡 **Open Redirect via SPA Router redirect_uri / returnUrl Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Capture the login redirect URL (e.g., /login?returnUrl=/dashboard). Replace the returnUrl value with https://evil.com or //evil.com and complete authentication. Observe whether the post-login redirect lands on the external domain.
  - signal: After authentication, the browser Location header or window.location.href contains the attacker-supplied external domain. HTTP 302 response Location value matches the injected URL rather than an allowed same-origin path.
- 🟡 **Sensitive Tokens Stored in localStorage / sessionStorage** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Open browser DevTools Application tab after authenticating. Inspect localStorage and sessionStorage keys for any entries whose values decode as JWTs (header.payload.signature), Bearer tokens, or API key patterns. Also observe Network panel Authorization headers to confirm what was read from storage.
  - signal: localStorage or sessionStorage contains a key (e.g., access_token, jwt, auth_token) whose value matches a JWT regex (^[A-Za-z0-9-_]+\.[A-Za-z0-9-_]+\.[A-Za-z0-9-_]+$) or an API key pattern. Presence is a finding; no active injection needed.
- 🟠 **Overly Permissive CORS on SPA API Endpoints** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send preflight and simple cross-origin requests to API endpoints (e.g., GET /api/user/me) with an Origin: https://attacker.com header. Check the response for Access-Control-Allow-Origin mirroring the attacker domain and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true simultaneously.
  - signal: Response headers contain both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://attacker.com (reflecting injected origin) and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true on an endpoint that returns sensitive data. Or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on any endpoint accepting cookies or Authorization headers.
- 🔴 **Angular Universal SSR — SSRF via Server-Side HTTP Fetches** — _A10:2021 Server-Side Request Forgery_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Identify URL parameters that appear to drive content fetching in SSR responses (e.g., ?src=, ?url=, ?apiUrl=). Replace values with http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ or http://localhost:9200/_cat/indices. Observe whether the server-rendered HTML contains cloud metadata or internal service data.
  - signal: Pre-rendered HTML response body contains cloud metadata strings (ami-id, instance-id, security-credentials/) or internal service signatures (Elasticsearch index listing, Redis info output) after submitting a crafted URL-scheme parameter. Response time increase >1 s vs baseline also indicates a live internal probe.
- 🟡 **Vuex / Pinia State Exposed via Vue DevTools Protocol** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: In the browser console, evaluate window.__VUE__ or window.__VUE_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__. If present, inspect ._instance.appContext.config.globalProperties.$store.state (Vuex) or enumerate Pinia stores via window.__pinia.state.value. No extension required.
  - signal: window.__VUE__ or window.__VUE_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__ is defined on the production page (truthy in DevTools console). Subsequent store state read returns auth tokens, session data, or PII objects. Also detectable by scanning the response JS bundle for the string devtools hook installed.
- 🔴 **JWT 'alg:none' Acceptance on SPA API** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Capture a valid JWT from localStorage or the Authorization header. Base64-decode and modify the header to {"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}, re-encode the desired payload (e.g., role: admin), append a trailing dot with no signature, and resubmit to protected API endpoints. Observe whether the server accepts the unsigned token.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response (or data change) on a privileged endpoint after submitting a forged alg:none JWT, where the original signed JWT with lower privileges returns HTTP 403. Response body reflects the tampered claim (e.g., admin: true in the JSON).
- 🟠 **Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) on SPA REST/GraphQL Endpoints** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as User A, capture API calls containing resource IDs (e.g., GET /api/orders/1042). Authenticate as User B in a second session, replay User B's token against User A's resource IDs. Alternatively enumerate IDs sequentially from the observed value.
  - signal: HTTP 200 with User A's data body when the request uses User B's Bearer token and User A's resource ID. Auth-differential comparison: same endpoint returns different ownership metadata (userId, email) for the two sessions, confirming cross-user access.
- 🟡 **Content Security Policy Misconfiguration Allowing Inline Script in Angular App** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Inspect the Content-Security-Policy response header (or <meta http-equiv=Content-Security-Policy>) on the SPA shell page and API responses. Parse the script-src directive for unsafe-inline, unsafe-eval, wildcard hosts (*), or the absence of CSP entirely.
  - signal: Response header Content-Security-Policy is absent; or contains script-src directives with 'unsafe-inline', 'unsafe-eval', or * wildcards. Observable purely from response headers of the initial page load — no additional requests needed.
- 🟠 **DOM-XSS via Vue Router Hash Fragment Reflection** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Append XSS payloads to the URL hash and named route parameters: /#/<script>alert(1)</script>, /#/page?q=<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>. Observe whether the Vue component renders the raw value into the DOM. Also test history-mode routes if hash mode is not used.
  - signal: After navigation to a crafted hash URL, browser DevTools Elements panel shows unescaped HTML in a Vue-rendered element (e.g., <h1> contains <script> as a child node rather than escaped text); or JavaScript executes. Console errors referencing 'vue-router' params alongside script execution confirm the vector.
- 🟡 **GraphQL Introspection Enabled in Production SPA Backend** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Send a POST to the GraphQL endpoint (commonly /graphql, /api/graphql) with the standard introspection query {__schema{types{name,fields{name}}}}. Check whether the response includes the full schema rather than an error or empty data.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response body contains __schema with a populated types array listing application-specific type names (e.g., User, Order, AdminPanel). Response Content-Type is application/json and the data.__schema.types array length is >10.
- 🟠 **Auth Token Leaked in Angular Error Logs / Sentry Breadcrumbs** — _A09:2021 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures_  ·  high/occasional/passive
  - discovery: Intercept outbound HTTP requests from the SPA using the browser Network panel. Filter for requests to known error-reporting endpoints (sentry.io, browser.sentry-cdn.com, rum.browser-intake-datadoghq.com). Inspect the POST body for breadcrumb objects containing Authorization, x-api-key, or Cookie header values from previous requests.
  - signal: Outbound POST to a telemetry/logging endpoint contains a JSON body with a breadcrumbs or data field that includes an Authorization or x-api-key value matching a token observed in the application's own API calls. Observable purely from passive network traffic interception.
- 🟠 **Client-Side Prototype Pollution in SPA Utility Libraries** — _A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit payloads containing __proto__[test]=polluted or constructor[prototype][test]=polluted through URL query parameters (e.g., ?__proto__[admin]=true) and form fields processed by vulnerable merge/assign functions. After submission, execute Object.prototype.test in the browser console to check if it equals 'polluted'.
  - signal: After submitting a __proto__ or constructor.prototype payload via URL parameter or form, browser console evaluation of ({}).test returns 'polluted' rather than undefined. In automated scanning, a subsequent request that reads from a property gated by Object.prototype returns unexpected truthy behavior (e.g., isAdmin becomes true on a non-admin object).

## Flask  (15)

- 🔴 **Jinja2 Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Inject SSTI probe strings ({{7*7}}, ${7*7}, #{7*7}) into every text input, query parameter, URL path segment, HTTP header, and cookie value. Observe response body for evaluated arithmetic. Follow up with class-chain payloads for RCE confirmation.
  - signal: Response body contains the literal evaluated result of the arithmetic probe (e.g., '49' when '{{7*7}}' was submitted). Distinct from XSS because the evaluation happens server-side before HTML rendering.
- 🔴 **Flask Werkzeug Debugger Exposed in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Trigger a 500 error by sending malformed input or accessing a nonexistent route. Observe whether the response contains the Werkzeug interactive traceback UI (HTML with 'Werkzeug Debugger' heading or the console icon). Also probe GET /__debugger__ and /console.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body contains 'Werkzeug Debugger', 'Traceback (most recent call last)', interactive console iframe, or the response contains 'X-Werkzeug-Debugger' header. The /__debugger__ endpoint returns 200.
- 🔴 **Flask Session Cookie Signed with Weak or Default SECRET_KEY** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Capture the session cookie (format: base64.base64.signature). Decode the header+payload (base64 with URL-safe alphabet, no padding). Attempt to re-sign with a wordlist of known weak keys using flask-unsign or manual HMAC. Submit the forged cookie and observe whether the application accepts the modified session data.
  - signal: A re-signed cookie containing {'role':'admin'} or an elevated user ID is accepted by the server with HTTP 200 and admin-level response content, rather than HTTP 401/403 or a session error page.
- 🟠 **Flask itsdangerous Algorithm Confusion (alg:none / RS→HS)** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Decode the session cookie or JWT. Craft a variant with the algorithm header changed to 'none' and the signature stripped. Submit to all authenticated endpoints and observe whether the server accepts the unsigned token.
  - signal: An unsigned (alg:none) or algorithm-swapped token is accepted by the server (HTTP 200 with user-specific content, no redirect to login), indicating the server does not enforce algorithm validation.
- 🟡 **Open Redirect via next / redirect_to Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Submit GET /login?next=https://evil.example.com and complete authentication (or observe the redirect even unauthenticated). Check Location header in HTTP 302 response for the external host. Fuzz variations: //evil.example.com, /\evil.example.com, %0d%0ahttps://evil.example.com.
  - signal: HTTP 302 response with Location header pointing to the attacker-supplied external domain (not just the path). Response does not normalize or reject absolute URLs with external hostnames.
- 🟠 **Insecure Direct Object Reference on Flask Route Parameters** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as user A, record the ID used in API calls (e.g., /api/orders/1042). Authenticate as user B in a parallel session. From user B's session, request user A's resource URL. Compare response bodies: if user B receives user A's private data with HTTP 200, IDOR is confirmed.
  - signal: Auth-differential check: user B's session retrieves user A's resource with HTTP 200 and identical private data fields (email, address, order details). Expected behavior: HTTP 403 or sanitized/empty response.
- 🟠 **Missing CSRF Protection on State-Changing Flask Endpoints** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: For each state-changing form or AJAX endpoint, submit the POST request without the CSRF token field (or with a tampered token). Observe whether the server accepts the request (HTTP 200/302) or rejects it (HTTP 400/403). Also check for missing SameSite cookie attribute.
  - signal: POST request without csrf_token field (or with token set to empty string) returns HTTP 200 or 302 with a success response body, instead of HTTP 400/403 with a CSRF validation error.
- 🟠 **Reflected XSS via Jinja2 Autoescape Disabled** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Inject standard XSS probe strings (<script>alert(1)</script>, <img src=x onerror=alert(1)>) into all input vectors (form fields, query params, URL path, headers that get echoed). Check whether the probe is reflected in the response body without encoding.
  - signal: Response body contains the unencoded probe string (literal < and > characters not converted to &lt;/&gt;), and the Content-Type is text/html. Script tag or event handler appears verbatim in the DOM.
- 🔴 **Werkzeug Debug PIN Derivable from Exposed System Info** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: After confirming debug mode is active, probe for LFI or path traversal to read /proc/net/arp, /proc/self/cgroup, /etc/machine-id, /sys/class/net/eth0/address. Also check whether error tracebacks expose the Flask app's filesystem path. Combine gathered values to compute the PIN formula offline.
  - signal: HTTP 500 traceback response exposes absolute filesystem path of the Flask app (e.g., '/home/app/app.py'). Additional LFI or SSRF probe returns /etc/machine-id or MAC address content, providing all ingredients for PIN derivation.
- 🟠 **Path Traversal via Flask send_file / Custom File Serving** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Locate file-download or static-serving endpoints (e.g., /download?file=report.pdf, /static/user-files/<name>). Submit path traversal payloads: ../../../etc/passwd, ..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd, and URL-double-encoded variants. Observe whether the response contains file content from outside the intended directory.
  - signal: Response body contains root: or /bin/bash (from /etc/passwd), or known sensitive file content (application.cfg, .env). HTTP status is 200 and Content-Type matches the served file type.
- 🔴 **SQL Injection in Flask/SQLAlchemy Raw Query** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Inject SQL probe payloads into search/filter query parameters and form fields: single quote ('), OR 1=1--, UNION SELECT NULL--, sleep(5). Observe for HTTP 500 database errors, response content changes, or response timing anomalies (for blind SQLi).
  - signal: HTTP 500 response containing SQLAlchemy stack trace or database error message (e.g., 'sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError', 'ProgrammingError', 'syntax error near'); OR boolean-based response body differs between ' OR '1'='1 and ' OR '1'='2; OR timing-based probe (sleep/pg_sleep) causes measurable response delay.
- 🟠 **Flask-CORS Wildcard or Credential-Permissive Misconfiguration** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send requests to API endpoints with a crafted Origin: https://evil.example.com header. Inspect the response for Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true together. Also check for * on the allow-origin header.
  - signal: Response headers contain both Access-Control-Allow-Origin matching the supplied attacker origin (not *) AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true simultaneously, or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on endpoints that set authentication cookies.
- 🟡 **Session Fixation — Session Not Rotated After Login** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: Record the session cookie value before login. Submit valid credentials. Record the session cookie value after login. Compare the two values: if they are identical, the session was not rotated and the app is vulnerable to fixation.
  - signal: The Set-Cookie header value for the session cookie is identical in the pre-authentication response and the post-authentication redirect response. No new session identifier is issued upon successful login.
- 🔴 **Flask Configuration / Environment Disclosure via Debug Endpoints** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Enumerate common Flask debug/admin paths: /_flask_debug, /admin, /config, /_debug_toolbar, /api/config, /env, /debug. Also check for Flask-Admin at /admin/ and flask-debugtoolbar JavaScript asset inclusion in page source which indicates the toolbar is active.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response body contains 'SECRET_KEY', 'DATABASE_URL', 'SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI', or other uppercase Flask config keys in JSON or HTML. Flask-Admin panel loads without authentication requirement.
- 🟡 **Flask Session Cookie Missing Secure / HttpOnly / SameSite Flags** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  medium/common/passive
  - discovery: Observe Set-Cookie headers in login and any authenticated response. Parse cookie attributes: check for absence of 'Secure', 'HttpOnly', or 'SameSite' directives. Also check that session cookies are not flagged with SameSite=None without Secure.
  - signal: Set-Cookie header for the session cookie is missing one or more of: Secure attribute (cookie transmitted over HTTP); HttpOnly attribute (not present in the header string); SameSite=Lax or Strict (absent or set to None without Secure). Observable purely from response header inspection with no active probing needed.

## Django / DRF  (15)

- 🟠 **Django Admin Interface Publicly Accessible** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the application for /admin/, /admin/login/, /django-admin/, /staff/. Issue GET requests and observe whether a Django admin login page (HTML containing 'Django administration' or 'django-admin' CSS class) is returned with HTTP 200.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response to GET /admin/login/ with response body containing the string 'Django administration' or a <form> with action='/admin/login/'. Absence of IP-restriction redirect (302 to a deny page) confirms exposure.
- 🟡 **DRF Browsable API Exposed in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Issue GET requests to known API endpoints (e.g. /api/, /api/v1/) with Accept: text/html. If DRF Browsable API is active, the response is a full HTML page with a 'Django REST framework' heading, navigation links, and form inputs for every listed endpoint.
  - signal: HTTP 200 HTML response containing the string 'Django REST framework' in the <title> or <h1> tag, plus a <nav> element listing API routes. Can be triggered just by visiting any DRF endpoint in a browser (Accept: text/html).
- 🔴 **DRF Missing Object-Level Permission (IDOR via API)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as User A, record object URLs returned from list endpoints (e.g. /api/orders/42/). Re-issue GET/PATCH/DELETE to those URLs while authenticated as User B. Compare HTTP status codes and body content between the two auth contexts.
  - signal: Auth-diff probe: User B (unrelated tenant) receives HTTP 200 with identical body to User A's response for /api/<resource>/<id>/ — or HTTP 200/204 on PATCH/DELETE that should have been 403. SequenceMatcher similarity > 0.9 between the two bodies flags the finding.
- 🟠 **Django DEBUG=True Enabled in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Trigger a 500-class error by submitting malformed input (e.g. a non-integer where an integer is expected on any detail URL), appending invalid query parameters, or requesting a non-existent object ID. Observe the response body for a Django yellow debug page.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body containing 'Django Version', 'Traceback (most recent call last)', 'Request Method', 'Request URL', and 'Settings' sections rendered as HTML. The string 'You're seeing this error because you have DEBUG = True' is a definitive signal.
- 🟠 **Django CSRF Exemption on State-Changing Endpoints** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit a state-changing POST/PUT/DELETE request to a session-authenticated endpoint without a CSRF token (omit X-CSRFToken header and csrfmiddlewaretoken field). If the server returns 200/201/204 instead of 403 Forbidden, CSRF protection is absent.
  - signal: POST request without X-CSRFToken header and without csrfmiddlewaretoken body param returns HTTP 200/201/204 on a session-cookie-authenticated request. A 403 with body 'CSRF verification failed' would indicate protection is active.
- 🔴 **DRF JWT Accepts 'none' Algorithm or Weak Secret** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Obtain a valid JWT from the login endpoint. Decode the payload (base64), modify the 'sub'/'user_id' to another user's ID, re-sign with algorithm='none' (empty signature), and replay to an authenticated endpoint. Alternatively, attempt HMAC brute-force with common secrets ('secret', 'django-insecure-*').
  - signal: An API endpoint returns HTTP 200 with a valid resource belonging to a different user_id when presented with a token whose header specifies 'alg':'none' and has an empty signature segment. Or a token signed with a guessable secret validates successfully.
- 🟠 **DRF Serializer Mass Assignment (Missing read_only / extra_kwargs)** — _A04:2021 Insecure Design_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Capture a POST/PATCH request to a resource creation/update endpoint. Inject extra fields in the JSON body (e.g. 'is_staff': true, 'role': 'admin', 'owner': <another user id>). Observe the response body or fetch the object afterwards to see if the injected value was persisted.
  - signal: POST/PATCH response body echoes back the injected privileged field with the attacker-supplied value, or a subsequent GET of the resource reflects it. HTTP 201/200 with the submitted value present in the JSON response confirms the finding.
- 🟡 **Django User Enumeration via Login/Password-Reset Timing or Message** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Submit login POST requests with a known-valid username and a known-invalid username, comparing response bodies and measuring response times. Repeat for /accounts/password_reset/ with a known email vs a random one and compare redirects or response text.
  - signal: Distinct response bodies between valid and invalid username/email submissions (e.g. 'Enter a correct username' vs 'Please enter a correct username and password'), or a statistically significant timing difference (>100 ms) between responses for existing vs non-existing accounts on repeated measurements.
- 🔴 **Django SECRET_KEY Leaked in Response or Headers** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Crawl the application for config-dump or settings-exposure endpoints (/api/settings/, /debug/, /env/). Trigger 500 errors via malformed input and inspect tracebacks. Search all observed responses for patterns matching Django's insecure key format or high-entropy 50-character strings.
  - signal: Response body containing a string matching the regex `django-insecure-[a-z0-9!@#$%^&*()]{40,}` or a JSON key named 'SECRET_KEY' with a high-entropy value. Also fires when the Django debug page HTML includes 'SECRET_KEY' in the Settings table.
- 🔴 **DRF List Endpoint Returns All Objects Across Tenants (Missing Queryset Scoping)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as User A and call a list endpoint (GET /api/users/, /api/orders/). Note object IDs returned. Authenticate as User B (different tenant) and call the same endpoint. Compare the two response bodies for cross-tenant objects appearing in both lists.
  - signal: Auth-diff: response bodies for User A and User B on the same list endpoint share one or more object IDs or contain objects whose owner/tenant field belongs to the other user. Body similarity > 0.5 with cross-tenant objects present is a strong signal.
- 🟠 **Django CORS Wildcard with credentials=true (django-cors-headers Misconfiguration)** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Issue a preflight OPTIONS request to an API endpoint with an arbitrary Origin header (e.g. Origin: https://evil.example.com). Inspect the response headers for Access-Control-Allow-Origin and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials values.
  - signal: OPTIONS response includes both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com (origin reflected or wildcard *) AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Wildcard with credentials is the highest-severity variant; reflected arbitrary origin with credentials also flags.
- 🟠 **Django Admin Custom Action Without Superuser Check** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Log in to /admin/ as a non-superuser staff account. Discover custom action names from the admin changelist HTML (they appear in the <select name='action'> dropdown). POST to the changelist URL with action=<action_name>&_selected_action[]=<id> to invoke the action directly.
  - signal: POST to /admin/<app>/<model>/ with action=<custom_action> and a selected object ID returns HTTP 302 (success redirect) rather than 403 when submitted under a staff-but-not-superuser session. The resulting database state change (observable via GET afterwards) confirms execution.
- 🟠 **Django Password-Reset Poisoning via Host Header Injection** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit a POST to /accounts/password_reset/ (or equivalent) with a spoofed Host header (Host: evil.example.com) and an email address you control. Examine the received password-reset email to see if the link domain matches the injected Host value.
  - signal: The application accepts the POST and returns HTTP 200/302 without rejecting the spoofed Host value (no 400 Bad Request or SuspiciousOperation response). Confirmation requires observing the emailed link, but the absence of host validation in the HTTP response is the runtime-observable signal.
- ⚪ **DRF 400 Validation Errors Leaking ORM Field Names and Choices** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  low/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Submit a POST/PATCH request with intentionally invalid or missing required fields and observe the 400 response JSON. Look for field names, choice lists, or relational identifiers (e.g. 'user_id', 'tenant_uuid') in the error detail keys that are not documented in the public API spec.
  - signal: HTTP 400 JSON response body with a top-level key structure mirroring the serializer's field names. Error messages like 'Invalid pk \"X\" - object does not exist' reveal FK field names and table existence. Presence of undocumented field names in error keys compared to the published OpenAPI schema is the flag.
- 🟡 **Django Session ID Not Rotated After Login** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: Capture the session cookie value from a GET to the login page (unauthenticated). Submit valid credentials via POST /login/. Compare the Set-Cookie session value in the POST response to the pre-login session value. If they are identical, session fixation is present.
  - signal: The sessionid cookie value in the POST /login/ response Set-Cookie header matches the sessionid cookie that was issued on the unauthenticated GET to /login/. Session rotation would produce a different sessionid value after successful authentication.

## FastAPI  (15)

- 🟡 **FastAPI Auto-Generated Docs Exposed in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Crawl the target; probe /docs, /redoc, /openapi.json, /api/docs, /api/openapi.json. Fetch /openapi.json and parse the JSON for paths, security schemes, and server variables. No auth required to read the schema on a misconfigured app.
  - signal: HTTP 200 on /openapi.json returns a JSON body with top-level keys 'openapi', 'info', and 'paths'. /docs returns an HTML page whose title contains 'Swagger UI'. /redoc returns a page containing 'ReDoc'. All are unauthenticated.
- 🟠 **FastAPI Route Missing Depends(get_current_user) Auth Guard** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl authenticated session to discover routes from /openapi.json. Then replay each request without the Authorization / Cookie header and compare HTTP status codes. Any route that returns 200 without credentials is a candidate finding.
  - signal: Authenticated request returns 200; unauthenticated replay of the identical request also returns 200 with the same or richer response body. Differential: auth-diff response bodies match or the unauthenticated body contains more data (not a 401/403).
- 🟠 **IDOR via FastAPI Path Parameter (Object-Level Authorization Missing)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as user A, record a resource ID from a normal response. Authenticate as user B (auth-diff), attempt to access user A's resource ID using user B's session. Compare response: if user B receives user A's data, IDOR is confirmed.
  - signal: User B's request to /users/<user_A_id>/profile returns HTTP 200 with user A's PII fields (name, email, etc.) rather than 403/404. Response body under user B's session contains fields identical to the response seen under user A's session.
- 🟠 **Pydantic Model Mass Assignment / Over-Posting** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit a POST/PUT request body that includes extra fields beyond the documented schema — specifically guessing privileged field names like 'is_admin', 'role', 'credits', 'verified'. Observe whether the response echoes the injected field or whether a subsequent GET reflects the modified value.
  - signal: POST body includes 'is_admin': true. Subsequent GET /users/me returns 'is_admin': true in the response JSON, or a privilege-gated endpoint that previously returned 403 now returns 200 with the modified session.
- 🟠 **Overly Permissive CORS (allow_origins=['*'] with allow_credentials)** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send a preflight OPTIONS request with an arbitrary Origin header (Origin: https://evil.example.com). Inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header. Also send a cross-origin request with Origin set and observe whether the response reflects it.
  - signal: OPTIONS response contains 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com' (reflected origin) alongside 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true'. Or GET response returns 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' on an endpoint that returns user-specific data.
- 🔴 **JWT Algorithm Confusion / None Algorithm Acceptance** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Obtain a valid JWT from a login response. Craft a modified token with 'alg': 'none' and no signature (trailing dot) with elevated claims (is_admin: true, role: 'admin'). Submit to protected endpoints. Also try re-signing with HS256 using the app's public key obtained from /openapi.json or /.well-known/jwks.json.
  - signal: A request bearing a forged 'alg: none' JWT receives HTTP 200 instead of 401/403. Or: a JWT re-signed with HS256 using the public key bytes is accepted. Response body matches a privileged user's data structure.
- 🟡 **Python Traceback / Debug Info Leaked in 500 Response** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Submit malformed inputs to every endpoint — type mismatches, boundary values, null bytes, oversized payloads, missing required fields. Look for 500 responses. Inspect the response body for Python exception text, file path patterns, and class/module names.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body contains 'Traceback (most recent call last):', absolute filesystem paths matching '/app/' or '/home/', or Python exception class names like 'sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError'. Content-Type is text/html or text/plain rather than application/json.
- 🔴 **SQL Injection via Raw Query Fallback in FastAPI/SQLAlchemy Routes** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Fuzz query string and body parameters with SQL metacharacters: single quote, UNION SELECT, comment sequences (-- , /**/), and boolean payloads (1=1, 1=2). Look for response content differences, error messages, or timing differences between true and false payloads.
  - signal: Boolean-based: payload '1 AND 1=1' returns full result set; '1 AND 1=2' returns empty set or different row count. Error-based: response body contains 'sqlite3.OperationalError', 'psycopg2.errors', 'ProgrammingError', or partial SQL statement. Time-based: SLEEP(5)/pg_sleep(5) causes >= 5 second response delay.
- 🟡 **Pydantic Type Coercion Bypasses Semantic Validation** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Submit requests with type-confused payloads: send a number where a string is expected, send a string for a boolean field ('true', '1', 'yes'), send an array where a scalar is expected. Observe whether the app accepts the coerced value and reflects it in business logic (e.g. a boolean field sent as 'admin' string).
  - signal: POST with 'active': 'yes' returns 200 and subsequent GET shows 'active': true. POST with 'amount': '100.00; echo pwned' triggers a 500 with database/processing error rather than a 422 validation error. Response diverges from the OpenAPI schema's documented type.
- 🔴 **SSRF via User-Controlled URL Parameter in FastAPI Endpoint** — _A10:2021 Server-Side Request Forgery_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Identify parameters named url, callback, webhook, redirect, endpoint, src, or target. Submit payloads targeting cloud metadata: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/, http://[::ffff:169.254.169.254]/, and Burp Collaborator/interactsh callback URLs. Observe response time, error messages, and whether response body contains cloud metadata content.
  - signal: Request with url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ returns HTTP 200 with body containing 'ami-id' or 'instance-type' strings. Or: response contains an internal hostname resolved successfully (DNS lookup observed on out-of-band callback). Or: a 500 with 'Connection refused' to an internal IP vs timeout to an external IP — timing/error differential reveals internal network topology.
- 🟡 **Missing Security Headers on FastAPI Responses** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Fetch any endpoint and inspect the response headers. Check for presence/absence of security headers. No active probing beyond a single GET request to the root and a few data endpoints.
  - signal: Response headers lack one or more of: 'X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff', 'X-Frame-Options: DENY' (or SAMEORIGIN), 'Content-Security-Policy', 'Strict-Transport-Security', 'Referrer-Policy'. Absence is the signal — purely header inspection on a normal response.
- 🟠 **No Rate Limiting on Authentication / Sensitive Endpoints** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send 50-100 rapid successive POST requests to /login or /auth/token with varying passwords. Observe whether the app returns 429 Too Many Requests at any point, introduces increasing latency, or locks the account. Absence of 429 across the burst is the finding.
  - signal: 100 consecutive POST /login requests all return either 200 or 401 — never 429. Response latency remains flat across the burst (no exponential backoff). No 'Retry-After' or 'X-RateLimit-*' headers appear in any response.
- 🟡 **API Token / JWT Passed in Query String (Server Log Exposure)** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  medium/common/passive
  - discovery: Crawl all endpoints and observe the URL patterns used for authentication. Check whether any authenticated endpoint is reachable with the token in the query string rather than (or in addition to) the Authorization header.
  - signal: Requests to /api/data?token=<jwt> or /export?api_key=<key> return HTTP 200 authenticated responses. The OpenAPI schema for the endpoint shows an APIKeyQuery security scheme. The full token value is visible in the request URL captured by the proxy.
- 🟡 **FastAPI BackgroundTask / Celery Task ID Enumeration** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as user A, trigger a long-running action (export, import, report generation) and capture the returned task_id.
