Authenticate as user B (auth-diff) and request /tasks/<user_A_task_id>/status or /jobs/<id>. Compare: if user B receives user A's task status or output data, IDOR is confirmed.
  - signal: User B's GET /tasks/<user_A_task_id> returns HTTP 200 with a result payload matching the input parameters submitted by user A (e.g. user A's email address, file name, or query parameters visible in the result). No 403/404 returned.
- 🔴 **Environment / Config Variables Echoed in FastAPI Health or Debug Endpoints** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Probe common diagnostic endpoints: /health, /healthz, /status, /debug, /info, /config, /env, /settings, /metrics, /_debug. Inspect response bodies for key-value patterns matching secret field names or high-entropy strings.
  - signal: GET /health returns a JSON body containing fields named 'database_url', 'secret_key', 'api_key', or 'password' with non-placeholder string values. Or response contains a high-entropy string (Shannon entropy > 4.0 bits/char) adjacent to a key name matching known credential patterns (key, token, secret, password, dsn).

## Ruby on Rails  (15)

- 🔴 **Mass Assignment — Privilege Escalation via Admin/Role Field** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Submit a registration or profile-update form and inject extra parameters: `user[admin]=true`, `user[role]=admin`, `user[is_staff]=1`. Compare the authenticated session's capabilities before and after; re-fetch the user profile endpoint as the same account to see if the field was persisted. Auth-diff by checking whether the modified account can reach admin-only routes that a normal account cannot.
  - signal: POST to `/users` or `/account` with appended params `user[admin]=1` returns HTTP 200/302 without a parameter-not-permitted error; subsequent GET to `/users/:id` or `/api/v1/me` returns `"admin":true` or `"role":"admin"` in the JSON response body, confirming the write was accepted.
- 🟠 **Default Catch-All Route Exposing Unintended Controller Actions** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Crawl the app and enumerate URL patterns by guessing controller/action pairs: `/admin/index`, `/users/destroy/1`, `/orders/export`. Look for any action name that differs from the REST standard seven (index/show/new/create/edit/update/destroy). Attempt HTTP GET/POST to guessed routes and observe whether a 200 or redirect-with-content is returned instead of a 404.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response body with HTML or JSON content for a guessed path like `/<controller>/<non-restful-action>` that is not linked anywhere in the crawled surface; the response differs from a genuine 404 page (different status code, different Content-Length, or recognizable application chrome).
- 🟠 **IDOR via Unscoped ActiveRecord Lookups (Direct Object Reference)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as User A, capture a resource URL such as `/invoices/42`. Authenticate as User B (different account), send a GET to `/invoices/41`, `/invoices/43`, `/invoices/1`. Auth-diff: if User B receives User A's data with a 200 instead of a 403/404, an IDOR is confirmed.
  - signal: User B's authenticated GET to `/resource/:id` owned by User A returns HTTP 200 with the resource body (name, email, data fields) that matches what User A sees; User B's own resource returns the same HTTP 200 structure, confirming no ownership enforcement.
- 🟠 **Strong Params `permit!` Whitelisting Everything** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Submit a form or JSON API body with extra fields commonly present in Rails user models: `[admin, role, confirmed, locked, stripe_customer_id, api_key]`. If a subset of those fields appear in the subsequent GET response or cause a behavior change (unlocked account, confirmed email without verification flow), `permit!` or an overly broad permit is in use.
  - signal: POST body containing `user[confirmed]=true` or `user[locked]=false` returns HTTP 200/302 and a follow-up GET to the user profile shows `"confirmed":true` or a changed field value; alternatively, the app does not return a Rails `ActionController::UnpermittedParameters` log error translated to a 400 for novel fields.
- 🟠 **Missing or Bypassable CSRF Protection on State-Changing Endpoints** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl all POST/PATCH/DELETE forms and API endpoints. For each, replay the request without the `authenticity_token` field or `X-CSRF-Token` header. If the server still processes the state change (returns 200 or 302 instead of 422 Unprocessable Entity), CSRF protection is absent or skipped.
  - signal: POST to a state-changing endpoint (password change, email update, account delete) without any `authenticity_token` parameter or `X-CSRF-Token` header returns HTTP 200 or 302 (success redirect) rather than HTTP 422 with a `ActionController::InvalidAuthenticityToken` error.
- 🟡 **Open Redirect via Unsafe `redirect_to params[:return_to]`** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Identify login, logout, and OAuth callback endpoints. Append `?return_to=https://evil.example.com` (or `?redirect=//evil.example.com`). After completing the authentication flow, observe whether the browser or the HTTP response Location header points to the external domain.
  - signal: HTTP 302 response Location header contains an external host (e.g., `https://evil.example.com`) after submitting `?return_to=https://evil.example.com` to a login or post-auth endpoint; the redirect target differs from the application's own hostname.
- 🔴 **SQL Injection via ActiveRecord String Interpolation** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: In search, filter, and sort query parameters, inject SQL payloads: a single quote `'`, `' OR '1'='1`, `1; --`, and `1 ORDER BY 1--`. For sort parameters specifically try `name ASC, (SELECT SLEEP(2))--`. Observe whether the response produces a Rails SQL error (500 with ActiveRecord::StatementInvalid), returns unexpected extra rows, or shows measurable time delays.
  - signal: Injecting `'` into a query parameter produces HTTP 500 with body containing `ActiveRecord::StatementInvalid`, `PG::SyntaxError`, or `SQLite3::Exception`; or time-based payload `'; SELECT pg_sleep(3); --` causes response time to increase by 3+ seconds compared to baseline.
- 🟠 **Reflected/Stored XSS via `raw`, `html_safe`, or Missing ERB Escaping** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit the string `<script>alert(1)</script>` (and URL-encoded/double-encoded variants) into every text input, URL parameter, and HTTP header that appears to be reflected in the response. Check name fields, search boxes, profile fields, and error messages. Also attempt stored XSS by saving the payload and retrieving it in a subsequent GET.
  - signal: The literal string `<script>alert(1)</script>` (unencoded, not `&lt;script&gt;`) appears in the HTTP response body with Content-Type `text/html`, indicating the value was rendered without HTML-escaping.
- 🟡 **User Enumeration via Devise Login/Reset Timing or Message Differences** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Submit the login form with a known-valid email and wrong password; record status code, response body text, and response time. Repeat with a non-existent email. Compare the two responses for any difference in message text (e.g., `Invalid email or password` vs `No account found`), HTTP status, or response latency (>100ms delta indicates BCrypt only runs for existing accounts).
  - signal: Two login attempts return different response body text or differ in response time by more than 150ms: known email + bad password produces one string (`Invalid email or password`) while unknown email produces another (`Email not found`), or the timing delta indicates BCrypt computation only occurs on the valid-account path.
- 🟠 **JSON API Mass Assignment via Unpermitted Nested Attributes** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: On endpoints that accept nested JSON (e.g., `POST /projects` with `included` or `relationships`), inject additional nested attribute blocks targeting associated models: `{"data":{"relationships":{"owner":{"data":{"id":"2"}}}}}`. Check whether the ownership of the created/updated resource changed to the injected ID by fetching the object afterward.
  - signal: After submitting a nested JSON payload with a foreign key or relationship ID pointing to another user's resource, a GET to the created/updated object returns the injected association ID (e.g., `owner_id: 2` rather than the authenticated user's ID), confirming the nested attribute write was accepted.
- 🔴 **Insecure Cookie-Based Session — Known/Weak Secret Key Base** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Capture the session cookie (`_session_store` or app-named cookie). Check if it decodes as a base64 JSON blob without encryption (older config) or a MessageEncryptor ciphertext. Attempt to decode the cookie using known default keys (e.g., `secret`). Separately, fuzz the session with a cookie containing serialized `{"warden.user.user.key":[[1],"$2a$..."]}`-style payloads to test whether an arbitrary user ID is accepted.
  - signal: Sending a crafted session cookie with a forged `user_id` returns HTTP 200 with response body containing the target user's email, name, or account data — confirming the signature was accepted and the server trusts the cookie value without re-validating identity.
- 🔴 **Path Traversal via `send_file` / `send_data` with User-Controlled Path** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Identify file download endpoints that accept a filename or path parameter (e.g., `/files/download?name=report.pdf`, `/attachments/123?filename=...`). Inject traversal sequences: `../../config/database.yml`, `%2e%2e%2fconfig%2fdatabase.yml`, `....//config//database.yml`. Check response Content-Type and body for YAML/env file content.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response with Content-Type `application/octet-stream` or `text/plain` whose body contains YAML keys like `adapter:`, `database:`, `password:` or environment variable assignments like `DATABASE_URL=`, indicating the server read a config file rather than the intended upload.
- 🟡 **Rails Verbose Error Pages Enabled in Production (config.consider_all_requests_local)** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: Trigger an application error by submitting malformed input to a known endpoint (e.g., passing a string where an integer is expected for an ID, submitting an invalid enum value). Also probe for 500s with boundary inputs like extremely large payloads, null bytes, and invalid UTF-8 sequences. Observe whether the error page contains a stack trace with file paths and source code.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body contains Rails error page markers: `<h1>` with the exception class name (e.g., `ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound`), a source code window showing `.rb` file lines, the full file system path under `/app/` or `/var/`, or the `Request Parameters` dump listing environment variables.
- 🟡 **Exposed `/rails/info/routes` and `/rails/info/properties` in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/occasional/active
  - discovery: Send unauthenticated GET requests to `/rails/info/routes`, `/rails/info/properties`, `/rails/info`, `/rails/mailers`. Also try `/rails/info/routes.json`. Check response for route table HTML (table with `Helper`, `HTTP Verb`, `Path`, `Controller#Action` columns) or a properties page listing `Ruby version`, `RubyGems version`, `Rack version`, `Rails version`.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response to `GET /rails/info/routes` with HTML body containing a table of controller action mappings (e.g., `users#index`, `admin#dashboard`), or `GET /rails/info/properties` returning a properties table showing `Rails version:` and `Ruby version:` values — confirming the info controller is accessible without authorization.
- 🔴 **Insecure Locale/Tenant Parameter Switching (Subdomain or `locale` Param Hijack)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as a user in tenant A (e.g., subdomain `acme.app.com` or `?account_id=1`). Then request the same resource endpoint substituting another tenant's identifier: `rival.app.com/dashboard` or `?account_id=2`. Auth-diff: if the response returns tenant B's data rather than a 403/404, the tenant boundary is not enforced at the data layer.
  - signal: Changing the `account_id` query parameter or subdomain in an authenticated request returns HTTP 200 with a response body containing a different tenant's organization name, user list, or data records — distinct from what the authenticated user's own tenant returns — confirming cross-tenant data access.

## PHP / Laravel  (15)

- 🟠 **Laravel APP_DEBUG=true Information Disclosure** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Trigger an error by sending a malformed request (invalid route parameter, bad JSON body, or a non-existent resource). Observe whether the response body contains a Whoops/Ignition stack trace page instead of a generic error page.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body contains 'Whoops\Something went wrong', 'Ignition', 'APP_KEY', full absolute filesystem paths like '/var/www/html/...', or SQL query strings with table names exposed in the HTML.
- 🔴 **Eloquent Mass Assignment Privilege Escalation** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Intercept a user registration or profile-update form POST. Inject extra fields such as is_admin=1, role=admin, or email_verified_at=2024-01-01 into the request body. Re-fetch the authenticated user profile and compare field values before and after.
  - signal: After injecting extra fields, the GET /api/user (or equivalent) response reflects the injected attribute (e.g., '"is_admin":true') or the app grants elevated permissions not available to a baseline account.
- 🟠 **Laravel Debugbar / Telescope Endpoint Exposed in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the app and probe /_debugbar/open, /_debugbar/assets/stylesheets, /telescope, /telescope/api/requests, and /telescope/api/queries without authentication. Also inspect HTML source for the debugbar injection div.
  - signal: HTTP 200 on /_debugbar/open?max=20 returns JSON with 'queries', 'session', or 'env' keys. /telescope/api/requests returns a JSON array of recent HTTP requests including headers and payloads.
- 🟡 **phpinfo() Page Exposed** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Probe common phpinfo paths: /phpinfo.php, /info.php, /php_info.php, /test.php, /?phpinfo=1. Also check server-info paths like /server-info and /_info.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response body contains the string 'PHP Version' followed by a version number and an HTML table with 'PHP Credits' or 'Environment' sections. The Environment table may contain DATABASE_URL, APP_KEY, or similar values.
- 🔴 **Laravel .env File Accessible via Web Root** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Probe /.env, /.env.backup, /.env.local, /.env.production, /.env.bak, and /.env.old directly with a GET request.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response with Content-Type text/plain or no content-type, and body contains lines matching APP_KEY=base64:, DB_PASSWORD=, MAIL_PASSWORD=, or AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=.
- 🟠 **Laravel Storage Public Symlink Exposes Private Files** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: After uploading or receiving a file as an authenticated user, note the file URL. Log out and attempt to access the URL unauthenticated. Also enumerate /storage/ for directory listing or probe predictable filenames (UUIDs, incremental IDs, YYYY/MM/DD/ paths).
  - signal: HTTP 200 response for /storage/<filename> when no auth cookie is present. Alternatively, HTTP 200 on /storage/ with directory index HTML listing filenames.
- 🔴 **PHP SQL Injection via Error-Based Leakage** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: For each input field and URL parameter, inject a single quote ('). Observe whether the response changes in status code, body length, or content. Try payloads like 1' OR '1'='1 and 1 AND 1=2. For error-based leakage, try ') OR extractvalue(1,concat(0x7e,database()))-- on MySQL targets.
  - signal: Response body contains 'You have an error in your SQL syntax', 'mysql_fetch', 'ORA-', 'pg_query()', or 'SQLSTATE[' error strings. Response body changes in structure between id=1 and id=1' indicating query manipulation.
- 🟠 **Laravel Route Missing auth Middleware (IDOR / Unauthenticated Access)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the app while authenticated to collect all API endpoint URLs. Then replay each request without the Authorization or session cookie. Compare response status codes and body sizes between authenticated and unauthenticated requests.
  - signal: Unauthenticated request to an API endpoint (e.g., GET /api/users/42, GET /api/orders/7) returns HTTP 200 with a full JSON body rather than HTTP 401 or 403.
- 🔴 **PHP Object Deserialization via unserialize()** — _A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Inspect cookies, hidden form fields, URL parameters, and API request bodies for base64-encoded or raw PHP serialized strings (starting with 'O:' or 'a:'). Replay the request after injecting a modified serialized object that references a known gadget class and observe for OOB interaction, timing difference, or error disclosure.
  - signal: Cookie or parameter value matches regex ^[OaCSb]:[0-9]+: (PHP serialize format). Injecting a malformed serialized string returns a distinct error ('unserialize(): Error at offset', 'Class not found') confirming unserialize() code path is reached.
- 🟡 **Laravel Sanctum / Passport Token Returned in API Response Body** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Perform a login POST and inspect the JSON response body for fields named token, access_token, or api_token. Then inspect the browser's localStorage (via JavaScript console or browser tools) for stored token values.
  - signal: POST /api/login response JSON contains a key access_token, token, or api_token with a value matching Laravel Sanctum token format (a long alphanumeric string or a pipe-delimited id|hash). localStorage contains a key with the same value.
- 🟠 **Laravel Overly Permissive CORS Configuration** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send requests to authenticated API endpoints with crafted Origin headers (Origin: https://evil.example.com) and observe the Access-Control-Allow-Origin and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials response headers.
  - signal: Response includes both 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com' (reflected) and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true', or 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' combined with session-cookie-authenticated endpoints returning data.
- 🟠 **PHP/Laravel Backup and Config Files Accessible** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: For every .php file discovered during crawling, probe common backup suffixes: .php~, .php.bak, .php.old, .php.swp, .php.orig. Also probe top-level paths: /backup.zip, /backup.tar.gz, /db.sql, /.git/config, /composer.json, /composer.lock.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response for a backup filename variant (e.g., /index.php.bak) with Content-Type text/plain or application/octet-stream and body containing PHP source tokens ('<?php', 'DB_PASSWORD', '$password'). HTTP 200 for /.git/config with [core] and [remote] git configuration.
- 🟠 **Insecure Direct Object Reference on Eloquent Auto-Increment IDs** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as user A and note the IDs in their resource URLs (e.g., /api/invoices/42). Switch to user B's session and request /api/invoices/41, /api/invoices/43, etc. Compare response content to confirm whether user B can read user A's data.
  - signal: Authenticated as user B, GET /api/invoices/42 (owned by user A) returns HTTP 200 with a full resource body rather than HTTP 403 or HTTP 404. Auth-differential comparison confirms different user context returns same data.
- 🔴 **Unrestricted PHP File Upload Leading to Webshell** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Submit a file upload form with a file named shell.php (or shell.php.jpg, shell.pHp) containing '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>'. After upload, navigate to the returned file URL and append ?cmd=id to test for command execution. Also try MIME-type bypass: send Content-Type: image/jpeg with a .php file.
  - signal: The upload endpoint returns a URL path ending in .php (or .pHp, .php5, .phtml). A GET request to that URL with ?cmd=whoami returns a response body containing a Unix username string (e.g., 'www-data') rather than a download prompt or image.
- 🟡 **Laravel Session Cookie Missing Secure/HttpOnly/SameSite Flags** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  medium/common/passive
  - discovery: Perform a standard login and inspect the Set-Cookie response header on the session establishment response. Check each flag: Secure (only sent over HTTPS), HttpOnly (not accessible via JS), and SameSite value (Strict/Lax vs None).
  - signal: Set-Cookie header for laravel_session (or XSRF-TOKEN) is missing the HttpOnly attribute, missing the Secure attribute when the site is HTTPS, or sets SameSite=None without also setting Secure. Observable via response header inspection without any active payload.

## ASP.NET  (15)

- 🟠 **ASP.NET Model Binding Mass Assignment (Overposting)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit every discovered form with appended hidden fields matching common sensitive property names (IsAdmin, Role, RoleId, IsActive, Balance, Price, ApprovedById, OwnerId). Observe whether the extra fields silently persist in subsequent GET responses, audit-trail entries, or API reads. Auth-diff: compare account state between a normal user who submitted the field and one who did not.
  - signal: POST with extra parameter (e.g. IsAdmin=true) followed by GET of same resource returns the mutated value, or a 200 OK with no validation error when the field should be server-side-only. A field absent from the rendered HTML form but accepted without a 400/422 response is a positive signal.
- 🔴 **ASP.NET ViewState MAC Disabled / Weak Machine Key** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Crawl all WebForms pages and extract __VIEWSTATE values from hidden inputs. Decode from Base64 and inspect the first bytes for an HMAC suffix. Attempt to submit a tampered ViewState (e.g., flip bits in the payload) and observe whether the server accepts it (200 OK) or rejects it (ViewState validation error / 500).
  - signal: Tampered __VIEWSTATE accepted (200 OK) with no MAC validation error in the response body. Alternatively, ysoserial.net-generated payloads returning an error that differs from a normal MAC failure (timing or error string change) indicates deserialization execution path was reached.
- 🟡 **ASP.NET Yellow Screen of Death / Detailed Error Pages Exposed** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Trigger exceptions by fuzzing route parameters with unexpected types (e.g., /product/abc instead of /product/123), submitting malformed JSON/form bodies, and sending requests with invalid Content-Type headers. Observe HTTP 500 responses for stack trace content.
  - signal: HTTP 500 response body contains strings like 'Stack Trace:', 'Server Error in', 'System.Web.HttpException', '[SqlException', or a full C# namespace path. Also look for the yellow-bordered ASP.NET error page HTML structure or Developer Exception Page HTML in production.
- 🟠 **ASP.NET Forms Authentication Ticket in Non-Secure Cookie** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/common/passive
  - discovery: After authentication, inspect all Set-Cookie headers for the .ASPXAUTH cookie. Check for absence of Secure attribute, absence of HttpOnly, SameSite=None or missing, and excessively long expiry times. Also probe for the cookie being sent over plain HTTP by making a forced HTTP redirect to the app.
  - signal: Set-Cookie header for .ASPXAUTH (or .AspNet.ApplicationCookie / .AspNetCore.Cookies) lacks Secure and/or HttpOnly flags. Or the cookie is returned on a plain HTTP (non-TLS) response. Also flag an unusually long Max-Age (e.g., 30+ days) for a session authentication cookie.
- 🟡 **ASP.NET Open Redirect via ReturnUrl / ReturnURL Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Find login forms by crawling for ReturnUrl/ReturnURL/returnUrl query parameters. Submit the login form with ReturnUrl=https://evil.example.com and observe the Location header in the 302 response. Also try ReturnUrl=//evil.example.com and ReturnUrl=/\evil.example.com to bypass path-prefix checks.
  - signal: HTTP 302 Location header pointing to an external domain (not the app's own origin) after a successful login. Also trigger if the redirect target starts with // or contains a foreign FQDN, even when preceded by /.
- 🟠 **ASP.NET Anti-Forgery Token Missing (CSRF)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl authenticated state-changing forms. For each form, resubmit the POST request with the __RequestVerificationToken field removed or replaced with a random value. Observe whether the server returns 400/403 (token validated) or 200/302 (token ignored).
  - signal: POST to a state-changing endpoint without a __RequestVerificationToken (or with an arbitrary token value) returns HTTP 200 or 302 and the action takes effect (record created/deleted, settings changed). A correctly protected endpoint should return 400 Bad Request or 403 Forbidden.
- 🔴 **SQL Injection via Raw Queries in ASP.NET (Entity Framework / ADO.NET)** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Inject SQL metacharacters (', --, ;, 1=1, WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5') into all form fields, query parameters, and route segments that are used for data lookup. Observe for database error strings in the response, unexpected data returns, or response-time increases (time-based blind).
  - signal: Response contains strings like 'SqlException', 'Unclosed quotation mark', 'Incorrect syntax near', 'System.Data.SqlClient', or 'ORA-00933'. Alternatively, boolean-based: injecting ' OR '1'='1 returns more rows than a baseline. Time-based: WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5' increases response time by ~5 seconds.
- 🟠 **Insecure Direct Object Reference via Sequential ID in ASP.NET Routes** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as user A and note resource IDs visible in responses. Then with user B's session (auth-diff) or even unauthenticated, request those same IDs. Also enumerate adjacent IDs (+1, -1). Observe whether the server returns 403/404 or the resource content.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response body containing another user's PII, order data, or account content when the requesting session does not own that resource ID. Auth-diff: resource returned to user B that was created by user A, with matching content.
- ⚪ **ASP.NET Version Disclosure via X-Powered-By / Server Headers** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  low/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Send any HTTP request to the application root and all crawled paths. Inspect response headers for X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version, X-AspNetMvc-Version, and Server header content.
  - signal: Response headers contain any of: X-Powered-By: ASP.NET, X-AspNet-Version (any value), X-AspNetMvc-Version (any value), or Server: Microsoft-IIS/<version>. Presence of specific version numbers (e.g., 4.0.30319) is a higher-severity signal than a generic framework disclosure.
- 🟠 **ASP.NET Session Fixation (ASP.NET_SessionId Not Rotated on Auth)** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Record the ASP.NET_SessionId value from a pre-authentication (anonymous) request. Submit valid credentials via the login form. Compare the ASP.NET_SessionId in the post-login Set-Cookie (or Cookie echo) with the pre-login value. If they are identical, session fixation is present.
  - signal: The ASP.NET_SessionId value in the post-authentication response is identical to the pre-authentication session ID. A secure app always issues a new session ID (new Set-Cookie with a different token) immediately after credential verification succeeds.
- 🟠 **ASP.NET CORS Misconfiguration — Wildcard or Reflected Origin with Credentials** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send requests with arbitrary Origin headers (e.g., Origin: https://evil.attacker.com) to all API endpoints. Inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header for reflection of the submitted origin combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.
  - signal: Response contains both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.attacker.com (exact reflection of probe origin) and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on endpoints that set authentication cookies. Either combination allows cross-origin session data theft.
- 🟠 **ASP.NET HTTP Verb Tampering — Authorization Bypass via Method Override** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: For protected endpoints (e.g., DELETE /api/resource/1 returns 401/403), retry as POST /api/resource/1 with header X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE or form field _method=DELETE. Also try X-HTTP-Method: DELETE and X-METHOD-OVERRIDE: DELETE. Compare response codes and side-effects.
  - signal: A request that returns 401/403 when sent as DELETE/PUT returns 200 or 204 when sent as POST with a method override header or form field. The side effect (record deleted, resource updated) is also present, confirming bypass rather than a response-code-only difference.
- 🟡 **ASP.NET Identity Username Enumeration via Differential Login Responses** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Submit login requests with a known-valid username + wrong password vs. a clearly nonexistent username + wrong password. Compare response body text, HTTP status codes, and response timing. Also compare password-reset flow responses for the same two cases.
  - signal: Differential response: body text differs (e.g., 'Invalid password' vs. 'Account not found'), or response time is measurably longer for valid usernames (indicating a bcrypt hash computation was attempted). Also flag if password-reset flow leaks 'If this email exists, we will send...' is absent for invalid emails.
- 🟠 **ASP.NET Path Traversal via Static File Middleware or Physical File Providers** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Probe known file paths using traversal sequences: /static/../appsettings.json, /files/..%2F..%2Fweb.config, /assets/%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fappsettings.Production.json. Also try URL-encoded and double-encoded variants. Observe whether the response returns 200 with file content vs. 400/404.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response body containing recognizable configuration file content: JSON with 'ConnectionStrings', 'Logging', or 'JwtSecret' keys (appsettings.json pattern), or XML containing '<connectionStrings>' or '<appSettings>' (web.config pattern). A 404 that suddenly becomes 200 on traversal is the key signal.
- 🟡 **Missing Security Headers on ASP.NET Responses (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/passive
  - discovery: Crawl authenticated and unauthenticated pages. For each response, inspect headers for presence of: Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options (or CSP frame-ancestors), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy. Score absence of each.
  - signal: One or more critical security headers absent from HTTP responses: no Content-Security-Policy on pages rendering user-controlled HTML, no Strict-Transport-Security on HTTPS endpoints, no X-Frame-Options (enabling clickjacking), or no X-Content-Type-Options. Multiple absences in combination elevate severity.

## Go web  (15)

- 🟠 **Missing or Misconfigured CORS in Go HTTP Handlers** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send a cross-origin preflight OPTIONS request with an arbitrary Origin header (e.g., Origin: https://attacker.example). Observe the response for reflected origin or wildcard in Access-Control-Allow-Origin combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.
  - signal: Response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin equal to the attacker-supplied Origin value AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true; or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on an authenticated endpoint (credentialed cookie present in session).
- 🟠 **net/http pprof Debug Endpoints Exposed on Default Mux** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl /debug/pprof/, /debug/pprof/goroutine, /debug/pprof/heap, /debug/pprof/cmdline without authentication. Check for HTTP 200 with profiling data.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response body at /debug/pprof/ containing 'goroutine', 'heap', 'allocs', or 'cmdline' index links; or /debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=1 returning a live goroutine stack dump.
- 🟡 **Go Runtime / Database Error Messages Leaked in HTTP Responses** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Trigger validation errors by submitting malformed input, invalid IDs, or non-existent resources. Observe 4xx/5xx response bodies for Go-specific error strings and internal details.
  - signal: Response body contains strings matching Go error patterns: 'sql:', 'pq:', 'no rows in result', 'dial tcp', 'connection refused', absolute Unix file paths (/home/*, /var/*, /go/src/), or goroutine stack trace markers like 'goroutine \d+ [running]'.
- 🔴 **Gin/Echo Route Groups Missing Authentication Middleware** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the application with two sessions: authenticated and unauthenticated. Compare the HTTP status codes and response body sizes for all discovered endpoints. Any endpoint returning 200 with substantial data for the unauthenticated session is a candidate.
  - signal: Unauthenticated request to an API endpoint returns HTTP 200 with a non-empty JSON body identical or similar in size/structure to the authenticated response, where the endpoint is not explicitly public (e.g., /api/v1/admin/*, /api/v1/users/*).
- 🟠 **HTTP Host Header Injection (Password Reset / Redirect Poisoning)** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Submit password-reset or email-confirmation requests with a modified Host header (Host: attacker.example). Observe whether the link in the email or the Location response header reflects the attacker-supplied host.
  - signal: The Location response header or response body URL contains the injected Host value verbatim. Alternatively, a redirect response (3xx) target host matches the probe value rather than the application's canonical domain.
- 🟡 **Open Redirect via Unvalidated URL Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Fuzz common redirect parameters (?next=, ?redirect_uri=, ?return=, ?url=, ?dest=) with an external URL (https://attacker.example). Follow the redirect chain and observe the final destination.
  - signal: HTTP 3xx response where the Location header value matches or starts with the injected external URL, or a redirect chain that ultimately lands on the attacker-supplied domain.
- 🟡 **Missing Security Response Headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Issue a GET request to the application root and any rendered HTML pages. Inspect all response headers for the presence of security-relevant headers.
  - signal: Response is missing two or more of: Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options (or CSP frame-ancestors), Referrer-Policy. Absence confirmed by header enumeration on 200 responses.
- 🟠 **Insecure Direct Object Reference via Sequential Numeric ID** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as user A, obtain a resource ID from a normal workflow, then enumerate adjacent IDs (id-1, id-2, id+1) as user B (or unauthenticated). Compare response body ownership fields.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response for an ID outside the authenticated user's known resource set, where the response body contains user-identifying fields (email, user_id, name) differing from the requesting user's identity.
- 🔴 **JWT 'none' Algorithm Accepted or HS256/RS256 Confusion** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Obtain a valid JWT, modify the header to alg:none and strip the signature, then replay the token to authenticated endpoints. Separately, attempt alg:HS256 with the server's public key as the HMAC secret.
  - signal: Authenticated endpoint returns HTTP 200 (or same data as a valid token) when presented with a JWT bearing alg:none and no signature, or alg:HS256 signed with the public RSA key bytes.
- 🔴 **Server-Side Request Forgery via URL Parameter in Go HTTP Client** — _A10:2021 Server-Side Request Forgery_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Submit URL parameters pointing to internal addresses: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (AWS), http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/, http://127.0.0.1:<common port>. Observe whether the response body contains metadata content or internal service responses.
  - signal: Response body contains cloud metadata markers ('ami-id', 'instance-id', 'computeMetadata', 'iam/security-credentials') or internal service banners; or response timing/size differs significantly from an invalid host, indicating a live internal connection.
- 🟠 **Path Traversal in Static File Serving (gin Static / echo Static)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Fuzz static-serving routes with URL-encoded and double-encoded path traversal sequences: /static/../../etc/passwd, /static/%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd. Observe response content-type and body.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response with body containing Unix passwd-file content ('root:x:0:0'), Go source file content ('package main'), or .env file markers ('DB_PASSWORD=', 'SECRET_KEY=') when traversal sequences are submitted.
- 🟠 **Missing CSRF Protection on State-Changing Endpoints** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Identify state-changing endpoints using session cookies. Submit cross-origin POST requests (via a crafted HTML form or fetch with no CSRF token) and observe whether the server processes the request successfully.
  - signal: HTTP 200 or 204 response to a state-changing POST/PUT/DELETE request submitted without a CSRF token header or body parameter, when a valid session cookie is present. Confirmed when the same request without the session cookie returns 401/403.
- ⚪ **Unrestricted HTTP Methods on Go Endpoints** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  low/common/active
  - discovery: Issue TRACE, OPTIONS, and arbitrary HTTP method (FUZZ, HACK) requests to all discovered endpoints. Observe which methods return non-405 responses. Check TRACE responses for cookie/header reflection.
  - signal: TRACE request returns HTTP 200 with request headers reflected in the response body (including Cookie and Authorization headers). Or OPTIONS response lists methods including TRACE/CONNECT/arbitrary verbs not expected for the endpoint.
- 🟠 **No Rate Limiting on Authentication and Sensitive Endpoints** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Send 50-100 rapid successive POST requests to /login, /forgot-password, or /verify-otp with varying credentials. Measure whether response time, status code, or body changes to indicate throttling or lockout.
  - signal: All 100 requests return the same HTTP status code and response time distribution with no 429 Too Many Requests, no Retry-After header, and no CAPTCHA challenge; response body remains consistent (no lockout message) across all attempts.
- 🟠 **Mass Assignment via Echo/Gin Struct Binding** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit POST/PUT requests with additional JSON fields beyond those expected by the API (is_admin:true, role:'admin', balance:9999999, owner_id:<other user>). Observe whether a subsequent GET of the resource reflects the injected values.
  - signal: A subsequent GET request for the resource shows the injected field value persisted (e.g., role field changed to 'admin', balance field reflects the attacker-supplied number), or the application returns elevated-privilege responses after the injection.

## BaaS + GraphQL  (15)

- 🔴 **Supabase Table Readable by Anon Key (RLS Disabled or No SELECT Policy)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Extract the Supabase URL and anon key from the app's JavaScript bundle or network traffic. Issue GET /rest/v1/<table>?select=*&limit=10 with headers `apikey: <anon_key>` and `Authorization: Bearer <anon_key>`. A 200 response containing rows confirms the table is unprotected. Enumerate table names from JS bundle, network requests, or the PostgREST schema endpoint (GET /rest/v1/ returns the OpenAPI spec listing all tables).
  - signal: HTTP 200 with a JSON array body from GET /rest/v1/<table>?select=*; row count > 0; column names visible in response body. The OpenAPI spec at /rest/v1/ enumerates all exposed table names.
- 🔴 **Supabase Table Writable by Anon Key (No INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE Policy)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: For each table discovered as accessible (or listed via the OpenAPI spec), send POST /rest/v1/<table> with a synthetic body and headers `Prefer: tx=rollback, return=minimal` and `Content-Type: application/json`. A 400 response (schema mismatch) instead of 403 proves INSERT access is enabled. For UPDATE, try PATCH /rest/v1/<table>?id=eq.999999 with a dummy column; 404 vs 403 distinguishes allowed-but-not-found from blocked.
  - signal: POST to /rest/v1/<table> returns HTTP 400 (column constraint / schema error) rather than HTTP 403 (RLS blocked). HTTP 201 or 409 also confirms write access. The distinguishing signal is any non-403 status when an unauthorized write is attempted.
- 🔴 **Supabase service_role Key Exposed in Client Bundle** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/passive
  - discovery: Intercept network traffic or inspect JavaScript bundles for long JWT strings (eyJ...). Base64-decode the JWT payload (second `.`-separated segment) and read the `role` claim. A value of `service_role` confirms the critical exposure. Also check `Authorization` headers in requests captured by a proxy.
  - signal: JWT payload decoded from a string found in JS bundle or network request contains `"role": "service_role"`. The token is distinguishable from the anon key because the anon key contains `"role": "anon"`.
- 🟠 **Supabase BOLA via Insufficient RLS Policy (Auth Filter Missing auth.uid())** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as two different test users (user A and user B). Both users create a resource (e.g., a profile or order). User A queries GET /rest/v1/<table>?select=* with their JWT. If the response includes rows owned by user B (identified by a `user_id` or `owner_id` column differing from user A's `auth.uid()`), the RLS policy has an IDOR/BOLA flaw. Automate by comparing the `user_id` column in results against the authenticated JWT's `sub` claim.
  - signal: Response to GET /rest/v1/<table>?select=* with user A's auth token includes rows where `user_id` != user A's JWT `sub` claim. Differential: user B's known resource ID appears in user A's result set.
- 🟠 **Supabase RPC Function Callable by anon Without auth.uid() Check** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Enumerate RPC function names from JS bundle network calls or the PostgREST OpenAPI spec at /rest/v1/. POST /rest/v1/rpc/<function_name> with empty body `{}` and the anon key. A non-403 response (200, 400 with schema error, 500 with internal error) indicates the function is reachable. Cross-reference with the function's JS invocation context to assess impact.
  - signal: POST /rest/v1/rpc/<fn> with anon credentials returns HTTP 200, 400, or 500 instead of HTTP 403. Function names containing 'admin', 'delete', 'update', 'transfer', or 'bulk' are highest priority.
- 🟠 **Supabase Storage Bucket Publicly Readable (No Storage Policy)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Issue GET /storage/v1/bucket with the anon key. The response lists all buckets and their `public` flag. For each bucket, issue GET /storage/v1/object/list/<bucket_name>?limit=10 with the anon key. A 200 response with an `items` array confirms object listing. For public buckets, directly fetch object URLs without any auth header.
  - signal: GET /storage/v1/bucket returns a JSON array where any entry has `"public": true`. GET /storage/v1/object/list/<bucket> with anon key returns HTTP 200 with an `items` array. Public object URLs are directly accessible without Authorization header.
- 🟠 **Supabase Realtime Channel Subscription Without Authorization (BOLA)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Intercept WebSocket traffic during app use. Observe the `realtime:channel` subscription message format. Extract the channel naming pattern from JavaScript source. Using a second authenticated session (user B), subscribe to user A's channel name (e.g., `user-<user_A_uuid>`). If messages intended for user A arrive in user B's subscription, the channel is unprotected. The channel name may also appear in the PostgREST schema or JS bundle.
  - signal: WebSocket subscription to a channel named with another user's identifier (extracted from profile API) succeeds (server sends `phx_reply` with `status: ok`) and delivers messages. JS bundle contains hardcoded channel patterns like `user-${userId}` or `private-${uid}`.
- 🔴 **Firebase Realtime Database Open to Public Read (No Security Rules)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Extract the Firebase database URL from the app's JS bundle (matches `https://<project>.firebaseio.com`). Issue GET https://<project>.firebaseio.com/.json?shallow=true (no credentials needed). A 200 response with non-null JSON data confirms public read. To confirm write access, attempt PUT to a test path (e.g., /.json path with a canary key). Firebase returns 200 on success or 401/permission_denied when rules block.
  - signal: GET https://<project>.firebaseio.com/.json?shallow=true returns HTTP 200 with JSON body containing top-level keys (not `null` or `{"error": "Permission denied"}`). Response time < 2s with data confirms live open database.
- 🟠 **Firebase Storage Publicly Listable Bucket** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Extract the Firebase storage bucket name from JS config (e.g., `<project>.appspot.com`). Issue GET https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/<bucket>/o?maxResults=5 without credentials. A 200 response with an `items` array confirms public listing. Attempt to download the first object's download URL. Check for sensitive filenames (receipts, invoices, private documents).
  - signal: GET https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/<bucket>/o returns HTTP 200 with `{"items": [...]}` array without any Authorization header. Object download URLs in the `downloadTokens` field are accessible publicly.
- 🟠 **PocketBase Collection Readable Without Auth (No API Rules Set)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Probe GET /api/collections (returns collection names when admin API is accessible). For each collection name, issue GET /api/collections/<name>/records?perPage=5 without any auth token. A 200 response with a `items` array and `totalItems > 0` confirms the collection is open. Also attempt the /api/health endpoint to confirm the PocketBase instance is live.
  - signal: GET /api/collections/<name>/records without Authorization header returns HTTP 200 with `{"items": [...], "totalItems": N}`. The `/api/collections` list endpoint returning 200 without admin credentials also indicates misconfigured admin API exposure.
- 🟡 **GraphQL Introspection Enabled on Production Endpoint** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Send POST to the GraphQL endpoint (typically /graphql, /api/graphql, /v1/graphql, /hasura/v1/graphql) with body `{"query": "{__schema{queryType{name}}}"}` and `Content-Type: application/json`. A 200 response containing `__schema` data confirms introspection is enabled. Follow up with a full introspection query to map all types and resolve mutation names.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response to POST /graphql with `{"query":"{__schema{queryType{name}}}"}` body contains `{"data":{"__schema":{...}}}`. Non-null `queryType` field confirms introspection is live. Hasura specifically returns a large schema JSON including `x-hasura-*` permission directives.
- 🟠 **GraphQL BOLA — Direct Object Reference via ID Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as user A and note the IDs of your own resources (e.g., order ID, message ID) returned in normal queries. Then query the same type by ID substituting known or guessed IDs of other users: `query { order(id: <other_id>) { id, user_id, amount, details } }`. If the response returns another user's data, BOLA is confirmed. In Hasura, compare query results with and without the `x-hasura-user-id` session variable.
  - signal: GraphQL query response for resource with ID not belonging to authenticated user returns `{"data": {"resource": {...}}}` with non-null fields and a `user_id` or `owner` field differing from the authenticated user's ID. Auth-differential (user A querying user B's ID) shows data in one session but not the other when access control is correct — if both return data, BOLA is present.
- 🟡 **GraphQL Query Batching / Alias Amplification (DoS / Brute-Force)** — _A04:2021 Insecure Design_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Send a POST to the GraphQL endpoint with a JSON array body containing 100 identical login mutations: `[{"query": "mutation { login(email: \"x\", password: \"y\") { token } }"}, ...]`. Alternatively, send one query with 100 aliased calls: `query { a1: user(id:1){id} a2: user(id:2){id} ... }`. If the server executes all operations and returns a 200 with an array of results, batching is enabled without restriction.
  - signal: POST to /graphql with a JSON array of N operations returns HTTP 200 with a JSON array of N result objects. Single query with N field aliases returns N resolver results in one response. Per-request rate-limit is not triggered. Response time scales linearly with N (confirms all operations executed).
- 🔴 **Hasura Admin Secret Exposed in Client Bundle or Headers** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Intercept all HTTP requests from the running app and inspect headers for `x-hasura-admin-secret`. Search JavaScript bundle content for strings matching Hasura admin secret patterns. Send a test GraphQL query with the discovered secret as the `x-hasura-admin-secret` header: if the response returns data that would normally require authentication, the secret is confirmed valid.
  - signal: HTTP request header `x-hasura-admin-secret` present in client-originating requests captured by proxy. String in JS bundle matching Hasura admin secret format. GraphQL query with the candidate secret returns HTTP 200 with `{"data": {...}}` instead of 401/403.
- 🟠 **Appwrite Collection With Guest Read/Write Permission** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Extract the Appwrite endpoint and project ID from the JS bundle (typical pattern: `https://<host>/v1`, `X-Appwrite-Project: <projectId>`). Issue GET /v1/databases with the project header only (no session token). A 200 response listing databases exposes configuration. For each database/collection ID found, issue GET /v1/databases/<dbId>/collections/<colId>/documents without auth and observe the response.
  - signal: GET /v1/databases/<dbId>/collections/<colId>/documents without `X-Appwrite-Session` or `X-Appwrite-Key` returns HTTP 200 with `{"total": N, "documents": [...]}`. Document entries contain `$permissions` arrays showing `role:all` or `role:guests` on read operations.

## AuthN / AuthZ  (15)

- 🟠 **IDOR / BOLA via Numeric Object ID** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Crawl authenticated as User A, collect all URLs/API calls containing an ID parameter. Re-issue each request incrementing or decrementing the ID value by 1, and also replay using User B's object IDs observed during a parallel User B session. Compare response body size, status code, and presence of PII fields.
  - signal: HTTP 200 with non-empty response body (matching schema of the original object) when the substitute ID belongs to a different user; or successful 200/204 on a PUT/DELETE that was only initiated with User A's session against User B's resource ID.
- 🟠 **BOLA via Non-Sequential (UUID) Object Reference** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Authenticate as a high-privilege user (admin), enumerate object UUIDs from listing endpoints. Then switch to a low-privilege session and replay GET/PUT/DELETE requests using those UUIDs. Also check Referer, Location, and Link headers, JavaScript source bundles loaded by the browser, and email notification URLs for leaked UUIDs.
  - signal: Low-privilege session receives HTTP 200 with full object payload (matching admin-visible schema), or 204 on a mutating request, when using a UUID harvested from an admin session.
- 🔴 **Broken Function-Level Authorization (BFLA) — Admin Endpoint Reachable by Regular User** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the app as an admin to collect the full URL inventory. Replay each admin-only API call (especially POST/PUT/DELETE to paths containing /admin/, /internal/, /manage/, /v*/admin) using a session authenticated as a regular user. Check for HTTP verb variations: if the UI uses GET, try POST/PUT/DELETE to the same path.
  - signal: Regular-user session receives 200/201/204 (not 401/403) on a request to a function that returned 200 for the admin session; response body or side-effect (e.g., resource deleted, role changed) confirms execution.
- 🔴 **JWT Algorithm Confusion — 'none' Algorithm Accepted** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Capture a valid JWT from the Authorization header or cookie. Decode the header and payload, modify the payload (e.g., set `role: admin` or change `sub` to another user's ID), set the header to `{"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}`, and reconstruct the token with an empty signature segment. Replay the modified token against protected endpoints.
  - signal: Server returns 200 with privileged content (or acts on the forged claim) instead of 401/403/signature-error when presented a token with `alg: none` and a tampered payload.
- 🔴 **JWT Algorithm Confusion — RS256 Public Key Used as HS256 Secret** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Fetch the server's JWKS endpoint (/.well-known/jwks.json or observed in JS source). Extract the public key (PEM). Craft a JWT with `alg: HS256` signed using that public key as the HMAC-SHA256 secret, modifying claims as desired. Present the forged token to authenticated endpoints.
  - signal: Protected endpoint returns 200 with valid user data (or elevated-privilege response) when presented an HS256-signed token that was forged using the server's RSA public key.
- 🟠 **JWT Weak HMAC Secret (Brute-Forceable)** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Capture a JWT from any authenticated response (Authorization header, Set-Cookie, or JSON body). Run the token through a wordlist attack (hashcat mode 16500 or jwt_tool). If the secret is recovered, forge a token with a modified sub/role claim and replay it.
  - signal: hashcat or jwt_tool recovers the signing secret from the captured token within a wordlist run; subsequently, a forged token is accepted by the server (HTTP 200 on a protected endpoint).
- 🟠 **OAuth Authorization Code Leaked via Open Redirect** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Initiate an OAuth flow and intercept the authorization request. Modify redirect_uri to a variant of the registered URI (subdomain swap, path traversal, appended path, URL-encoded characters). Observe whether the authorization server redirects the code to the modified URI instead of rejecting with an invalid_redirect_uri error.
  - signal: Authorization server issues a redirect to the attacker-controlled redirect_uri (HTTP 302 Location header contains the manipulated URI with `code=` parameter present), rather than returning an error response.
- 🟠 **OAuth CSRF — Missing or Unvalidated State Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Initiate an OAuth flow, capture the authorization URL, and inspect it for the `state` parameter. If absent, attempt a CSRF attack by forwarding a crafted callback URL with a known `code` to the victim. If state is present, replay the callback omitting state or with a substituted static value and observe whether the server accepts it.
  - signal: Callback endpoint processes the authorization code (HTTP 200/302 redirect to authenticated area) when state parameter is absent or does not match the session-bound nonce, indicating no CSRF validation.
- 🟠 **Session Fixation** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Obtain a pre-authentication session token (visit the login page without authenticating). Record the session ID. Complete the login flow. Inspect the post-login Set-Cookie header: if the session ID is identical to the pre-auth value, the app is vulnerable. Additionally, test whether the app accepts a session ID supplied in a URL parameter and promotes it to a cookie.
  - signal: The session cookie value in the post-login Set-Cookie response is identical to the session cookie value present before authentication (diff of pre-auth vs post-auth session token shows no change).
- 🟠 **Predictable or Short-Lived Password Reset Token Brute-Force** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Trigger password resets for a controlled email address multiple times in quick succession. Examine the reset tokens received: check length, character set, and whether tokens share a common prefix or are sequential. Attempt to decode as Base64/hex and look for recognizable fields (timestamp, username hash). Test if an expired token is still accepted after use.
  - signal: Reset tokens are fewer than 128 bits of entropy; or tokens show sequential/predictable patterns across multiple requests; or a previously used/expired token still returns 200 on the reset confirmation endpoint instead of an error.
- 🟠 **Password Reset Poisoning via Host Header Injection** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Trigger a password reset for a target account. Intercept the request and inject a modified Host header (or add X-Forwarded-Host) pointing to a controlled domain. Observe whether the resulting reset email contains a link to that injected domain, or use the Burp Collaborator / out-of-band DNS callback to detect the token being fetched.
  - signal: Out-of-band DNS/HTTP callback to the injected host receives a GET request with the victim's reset token as a query parameter; or the reset email (checked via test account) contains a link with the injected domain instead of the app's canonical domain.
- 🔴 **OTP / 2FA Code Returned in API Response** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/passive
  - discovery: Trigger an OTP send action (login with valid credentials, registration, phone verification). Inspect the raw HTTP response body and headers for numeric strings matching the OTP length (4-8 digits), or JSON keys named `otp`, `code`, `token`, `pin`, `verification_code`, `debug`. Also check Location headers and cookies set at this step.
  - signal: HTTP response body or header contains a field whose value matches the exact OTP that completes the 2FA challenge (confirmed by using the extracted value to pass the 2FA step without receiving an SMS).
- 🟠 **2FA Bypass by Directly Accessing Post-Auth Endpoint** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Complete step 1 of login (credentials accepted, 2FA prompt shown). Capture the session cookie issued at this point. Without completing 2FA, replay requests to authenticated-only endpoints (dashboard, profile, API) using that partial session. Observe whether the server grants full access or enforces 2FA completion.
  - signal: Requests to authenticated-only endpoints using the partial (pre-2FA) session token return HTTP 200 with full user data instead of redirecting to the 2FA prompt or returning 401/403.
- 🟠 **2FA OTP Brute-Force — No Rate Limiting or Lockout** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: After reaching the 2FA OTP entry screen with a valid partial session, send automated POST requests to the OTP submission endpoint cycling through all possible values (0000–9999 for 4-digit, or a subset for 6-digit). Measure whether the server returns 429 Too Many Requests, locks the session, or continues returning 401/400 without restriction.
  - signal: Server returns consistent 401/400 (wrong OTP) responses across 100+ sequential attempts without a 429, redirect to lockout page, or increasing delay; confirms absence of rate limiting.
- 🔴 **OIDC Account Takeover via Unverified email_verified Claim** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Register an account at the OAuth/OIDC Identity Provider using the target email without verifying it (some providers allow this). Complete the OIDC flow with the app. Inspect the ID token JWT claims for `email_verified` field. Observe whether the app accepts the login and maps to the victim's existing account.
  - signal: App completes login and opens the victim's account session when presented with an OIDC ID token containing `email_verified: false` (or missing the field) for an email address that corresponds to an existing app account.

## Injection (fuzz)  (14)

- 🔴 **Error-Based SQL Injection** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Fuzz every user-supplied input (GET/POST params, path segments, JSON keys, cookie values, HTTP headers that land in queries) with single-quote, double-quote, comment sequences (--,--, #), and stacked-query delimiters. Compare HTTP response body, status code, and content-length against the benign baseline.
  - signal: Response body contains DB-engine error strings: 'you have an error in your SQL syntax', 'ORA-00907', 'pg_query()', 'Unclosed quotation mark', 'SQLSTATE[42000]', 'Microsoft OLE DB Provider for SQL Server', or an HTTP 500 with a stack trace that names a DB library. Response content-length diverges meaningfully from the baseline.
- 🔴 **Boolean-Blind SQL Injection** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: For each fuzzable parameter, send three requests: benign, always-true condition appended (' AND '1'='1), always-false condition appended (' AND '1'='2). Compare body content, content-length, element counts, and DOM structure across the three responses.
  - signal: True-condition response is semantically identical to the benign baseline (same records rendered, same content-length ± a few bytes), while the false-condition response differs structurally (empty result set, 'no results found', zero-length list, missing DOM section). Consistent bifurcation across multiple parameter values confirms injection.
- 🔴 **Time-Based Blind SQL Injection** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Inject time-delay payloads into each parameter: MySQL: ' AND SLEEP(5)--, MSSQL: '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'--, PostgreSQL: '; SELECT pg_sleep(5)--. Record baseline RTT for each endpoint, then measure delta. Run at least 3 rounds per payload to filter network jitter.
  - signal: Response latency for the injected request exceeds baseline by ≥4 seconds in a statistically consistent manner (all 3 rounds delayed). Latency scales linearly with the sleep argument (1s, 3s, 5s). No change in response body or status code between injected and benign requests.
- 🔴 **NoSQL Operator Injection (MongoDB / DocumentDB)** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: For login forms and search endpoints accepting JSON, replace string field values with operator objects: {'username': {'$ne': ''}, 'password': {'$ne': ''}}. Also try query-string form: ?username[$ne]=&password[$ne]=. Observe whether the app logs in, returns a 200 with user data, or behaves differently than when correct credentials are supplied.
  - signal: HTTP 200 with a valid session cookie or auth token returned for the $ne payload when no valid credentials were sent. Alternatively, a search endpoint returns all documents instead of zero results when the $ne or $gt operator is injected into a filter field. Response body size is significantly larger than expected for a 'not found' case.
- 🔴 **Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Inject polyglot probe strings into all user-visible text fields (search boxes, profile fields, feedback forms, email templates, report names): {{7*7}}, ${7*7}, #{7*7}, <#assign x=7*7>${x}, *{7*7}. Submit and inspect the rendered HTML response for the evaluated result (49). Use a secondary canary string to distinguish reflection from coincidence.
  - signal: The literal string '49' (or engine-specific evaluated output) appears in the response body in place of the probe expression. The expression is not echo-reflected verbatim — it has been evaluated. Confirmed by varying the multiplier (e.g., {{7*8}} yields 56) to rule out static content matches.
- 🔴 **OS Command Injection** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Inject shell metacharacters with time-delay payloads into parameters likely passed to OS commands (filename, host, ip, domain, format fields): ; sleep 5, | sleep 5, && sleep 5, $(sleep 5), `sleep 5`. Measure response latency delta vs baseline. For OOB confirmation, inject DNS callback payloads (ping -c1 <collaborator_host>) and monitor the listener.
  - signal: Time-delay: response latency consistently exceeds baseline by ≥4 seconds for sleep-based payloads. OOB: DNS or HTTP request received at the Burp Collaborator / interactsh listener with the app's source IP. Error-based: response contains shell output fragments like uid=, /etc/passwd lines, or Windows system directory listings when whoami/dir is injected.
- 🔴 **XML External Entity (XXE) Injection** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Identify endpoints that consume XML (file uploads, SOAP, XML API bodies, SVG uploads, DOCX/XLSX imports). Replace or inject a DOCTYPE block into the XML: <!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>]> and reference &xxe; in the body. Submit and inspect response. For blind XXE, use an out-of-band SYSTEM entity pointing to a Burp Collaborator HTTP URL.
  - signal: In-band: response body contains the contents of /etc/passwd (root:x:0:0:), /etc/hostname, or Windows system files. OOB: HTTP or DNS interaction received at the collaborator listener with the request originating from the app server's IP. Error-based: XML parser error message reveals local file path or network connectivity to internal hosts.
- 🔴 **Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** — _A10:2021 Server-Side Request Forgery_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Identify parameters that accept URLs or hostnames (webhook, avatar_url, callback, feed, import, preview, pdf_url, fetch). Substitute values with: (1) Burp Collaborator/interactsh URL to confirm OOB fetch, (2) http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ for cloud metadata, (3) http://localhost:6379/ for internal Redis, (4) http://[::1]:8080/admin for IPv6 loopback bypass.
  - signal: OOB: HTTP request received at collaborator listener from the app server IP. In-band: response body or error message contains cloud metadata content (ami-id, instance-type, IAM role credentials), internal service banners (Redis -ERR, SSH-2.0-), or internal IP addresses. Timing difference when requesting closed vs open internal ports (TCP connect timing oracle).
- 🟡 **Open Redirect** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Enumerate all redirect-like parameters in login flows, logout handlers, OAuth callbacks, and navigation links. Replace the value with an external URL (https://evil.example.com) and an OOB collaborator URL. Follow the redirect chain with a headless browser or intercept at the proxy level. Also try protocol-relative (//evil.example.com) and bypass encodings (%2F%2F, ///evil.example.com).
  - signal: HTTP 301/302/307 Location header points to an attacker-controlled domain. Or a meta-refresh tag or JavaScript window.location assignment in the response body contains the injected URL. For JavaScript URIs: Location: javascript:alert(1) received in a 302 response. OOB: HTTP request arrives at collaborator from browser/server after redirect is followed.
- 🟠 **Path Traversal (Directory Traversal)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Fuzz filename, path, file, doc, resource, template, page, and similar parameters with traversal sequences: ../../../etc/passwd, ..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd, ....//....//etc/passwd, %252e%252e%252fetc%252fpasswd. Also test path segments in REST-style URLs (/files/../../etc/passwd). Use both Unix (/etc/passwd) and Windows (..\..\windows\win.ini) targets.
  - signal: Response body contains file contents matching known system files: root:x:0:0: from /etc/passwd, [boot loader] from boot.ini, [extensions] from win.ini, or base64-encoded content of a source file. HTTP 200 with content-type text/plain or application/octet-stream for a traversal-based path. Response size differs substantially from the 404/error baseline.
- 🟠 **Mass Assignment / Parameter Binding** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Intercept account registration, profile update, and object-creation requests. Replay them with additional JSON/form fields: role=admin, is_admin=true, admin=1, group_id=1, balance=99999, verified=true, owner_id=<other_user_id>. Compare the application state after submission (re-fetch the profile/object) against the state before. Also probe nested objects: {user: {role: 'admin'}}.
  - signal: After replaying the modified request, the subsequent GET of the profile/object endpoint returns the injected field value (e.g., role: 'admin', balance: 99999). HTTP 200 response to the modified write request (no validation rejection). Auth-diff: re-fetching the resource as the same user now reveals elevated permissions or changed attribute values not achievable through the normal UI.
- 🟠 **Host Header Injection** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: On password-reset and email-verification flows, replay the request with a modified Host header (Host: evil.example.com) or supplementary headers (X-Forwarded-Host, X-Host, X-Original-Host, Forwarded: host=evil.example.com). Trigger a password-reset email to a test account and inspect the link domain. Also probe for cache poisoning by injecting a Host and observing whether the modified value is reflected in the response or stored in a cache.
  - signal: Password-reset or account-confirmation email contains a link pointing to the attacker-supplied host value instead of the legitimate domain. Alternatively: the HTTP response body or Location header reflects the injected Host value (X-Forwarded-Host reflection in an HTML link, canonical URL, or CORS header). Cache-poisoning variant: second request with a legitimate Host header returns a response containing the previously injected evil domain.
- 🔴 **SSRF via Cloud Instance Metadata Endpoint** — _A10:2021 Server-Side Request Forgery_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: On any URL-fetching parameter, inject http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ (AWS), http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/ with Metadata-Flavor: Google header smuggled via SSRF, or http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01 (Azure). Use DNS rebinding or URL parser confusion bypasses (http://[::ffff:169.254.169.254]/, http://169.254.169.254.nip.io/) when direct IP is blocked.
  - signal: Response body contains IAM credential JSON with keys AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, Token; GCP service account token with access_token; or Azure managed identity token with access_token. Even partial metadata exposure (instance-id, region, account-id) confirms the SSRF vector and indicates IMDSv2 is not enforced or bypassable.
- 🔴 **Blind SSTI via Out-of-Band DNS/HTTP Callback** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: Inject OOB callback expressions into fields whose output is not immediately visible (email subject, report title, notification message, audit description): Jinja2: {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()[X]('curl http://<collab>',shell=True,stdout=-1).communicate()}}, FreeMarker: <#assign ex='freemarker.template.utility.Execute'?new()>${ex('curl http://<collab>')}. Monitor the Burp Collaborator/interactsh listener for HTTP/DNS interactions.
  - signal: HTTP or DNS request received at the out-of-band listener with source IP matching the app server within seconds of the probe submission. The request path or hostname contains a per-request nonce embedded in the payload, ruling out false positives from unrelated crawlers. No change in the HTTP response body or status is required for confirmation.

## Client / browser  (15)

- 🟠 **Reflected XSS** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Inject a benign HTML marker (e.g., <kbd>keyleak-xss-probe-UNIQUEID</kbd> or <b>keyleak</b>) into every parameter of every crawled form and URL query string. If the marker appears unescaped in the response body (confirmed by parsing the DOM, not just string search), escalate to a script payload. Compare reflected value across HTML context, attribute context, and JS string context.
  - signal: Injected marker string appears verbatim in the DOM response with HTML tag structure intact (status 200); DOMParser confirms it as an element node rather than a text node. Any occurrence of <script>, onerror=, onload=, or javascript: derived from probe input in the response.
- 🟠 **Stored XSS** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Submit a benign but unique HTML marker through every write-capable form field (comment box, display name, address line, description). Then re-fetch the page or report view as a different authenticated user (auth-diff scan). If the marker appears unencoded in the second user's DOM, it is stored XSS. Check rendered HTML, not just raw HTTP body, to catch cases where SSR escapes but client-side frameworks re-inject.
  - signal: Probe string submitted by User A appears as an unescaped HTML node (not a text node) in the DOM fetched by User B. Alert or script-execution event fires in headless browser. The response page contains the exact probe token in an element or attribute position.
- 🟠 **DOM-Based XSS** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Inject XSS payloads in the URL fragment (#) and query string, then observe the page with a headless browser for alert/console-error callbacks or DOM node insertion. Enumerate event listeners on message events and send synthetic postMessage payloads containing HTML. Observe DOM state before and after controlled input via page.evaluate.
  - signal: Headless browser fires an alert or throws a controlled error after injected fragment/query payload. DOM snapshot contains injected HTML as an element node after JavaScript execution. Source-map scan reveals innerHTML/outerHTML assignment from location.hash or message.data (pattern already in detectors.py xss_sink_lead).
- 🟠 **CORS Misconfiguration (Credentialed Cross-Origin Read)** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send OPTIONS and GET requests to every discovered API endpoint with a synthetic Origin header (e.g., Origin: https://attacker.keyleak.test). Inspect response for Access-Control-Allow-Origin reflecting the probe value AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Also probe null origin (Origin: null) which some CORS stacks allow.
  - signal: Response header Access-Control-Allow-Origin matches the injected probe origin exactly (not a wildcard) while Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is also present. Or: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on an endpoint that also sets a session cookie. Any reflection of the Origin: null value.
- 🟡 **Missing or Weak Content Security Policy** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Inspect the Content-Security-Policy and Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only response headers on every crawled page response. Parse the policy directives to detect absent script-src, presence of unsafe-inline or unsafe-eval, wildcard (*) sources in script-src or default-src, or missing upgrade-insecure-requests.
  - signal: Response has no CSP header at all; or script-src contains 'unsafe-inline', 'unsafe-eval', or a wildcard host (https://*); or default-src is * with no restrictive script-src override. Passive observation of response headers only — no active probe needed.
- 🟠 **Client-Side Prototype Pollution** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Fetch each crawled URL and append a probe query string: ?__proto__[keyleaktest]=polluted and ?constructor[prototype][keyleaktest]=polluted. After page load, execute page.evaluate(() => ({}).keyleaktest) in the headless browser context. A non-undefined return value confirms pollution. Also fuzz JSON request bodies on API endpoints with {"__proto__":{"keyleaktest":true}}.
