  - signal: page.evaluate returns a non-undefined value for a sentinel property name seeded only via the probe parameter (e.g., ({}).keyleaktest === 'polluted'). Console errors referencing prototype or constructor mutations are also a secondary signal.
- 🟠 **postMessage Origin Validation Bypass** — _A03:2021 Injection_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Enumerate message event listeners by injecting a script that captures addEventListener('message', ...) registrations and logs the handler source. Send synthetic window.postMessage payloads from the scanner context with a crafted origin and data payload containing HTML/JS. Observe DOM changes, navigation events, or data exposure after message receipt.
  - signal: DOM mutation or navigation occurs after a synthetic postMessage from a non-matching origin. Source-map or bundle scan finds message event handler that either lacks origin check or uses string.includes/regex match (xss_sink_lead pattern triggers on message.data reaching innerHTML). Headless browser fires an alert or script executes following the probe message.
- 🟠 **Exposed JavaScript Source Maps with Embedded Secrets** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl all JS bundle URLs loaded by each page. For each bundle, extract the sourceMappingURL comment. Fetch the referenced .map file (same-origin check). Parse the source map and run credential-pattern detectors against each reconstructed source file. Also probe for .map by appending .map to every bundle URL even if no directive is present.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response for <bundle>.js.map with a valid JSON source map body (version:3, sources array, sourcesContent). Credential detector patterns (API keys, tokens, DB URLs) match inside reconstructed sourcesContent entries. The sourceMappingURL is present in the bundle but the .map endpoint is unprotected.
- 🟡 **Clickjacking (Missing Frame Ancestor Protection)** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: For every page returned with HTTP 200, inspect the X-Frame-Options header (must be DENY or SAMEORIGIN) and the Content-Security-Policy header for a frame-ancestors directive. Attempt to embed the target URL in a data-URI iframe from the scanner's headless browser context and observe whether the frame loads.
  - signal: Response header X-Frame-Options is absent or set to ALLOWALL; CSP frame-ancestors is absent or set to *; headless browser successfully renders the target page inside a cross-origin iframe without error. Pages containing form[action] or button[type=submit] that load in the iframe are higher priority.
- 🟡 **Sensitive Credentials / Tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  medium/common/passive
  - discovery: After authenticating and loading each crawled page, execute page.evaluate to enumerate all localStorage and sessionStorage keys and values. Run credential-pattern detectors (JWT regex, API key patterns, email/SSN patterns) against every key-value pair. Also check IndexedDB stores and Cache Storage for the same patterns.
  - signal: localStorage or sessionStorage contains a value matching a known token pattern (JWT: eyJ..., Bearer token, API key prefix like sk-, AIza, AKIA). Scanner's browser_scanner.py already enumerates storage and runs detector patterns — the finding source will be localStorage:<key> or sessionStorage:<key>.
- 🟠 **Sensitive Data Rendered into DOM / Hidden Fields** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  high/common/passive
  - discovery: Crawl and render each page, then scan the full document.documentElement.outerHTML (and all inline script text) against credential and PII detector patterns. Pay particular attention to <script>window.__INITIAL_STATE__=...</script>, <input type=hidden>, data-token=, and meta[name=csrf-token] attributes. Run as both unauthenticated and authenticated users to compare exposure.
  - signal: Credential-pattern match (API key, JWT, private key PEM, SSN, credit card number) found in the raw DOM string at a source prefixed 'document'. Finding type source_map_reference or xss_sink_lead does not cover this — it is a distinct leak:dom_secret finding class. Inline JSON blobs containing keys like apiKey, accessToken, secretKey trigger the match.
- 🟡 **Open Redirect via Client-Side URL Parameter** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Enumerate all URL parameters and form fields named next, redirect, return, returnUrl, url, to, goto, dest, target. Substitute the value with https://attacker.keyleak.test and observe via headless browser whether navigation to that domain occurs or a 3xx Location header points there. Also probe with relative paths like //attacker.keyleak.test to bypass naive scheme checks.
  - signal: Navigation event fires in the headless browser to an origin other than the target app's origin after injecting the probe URL into a recognized redirect parameter. HTTP 302/303 Location header contains the injected external URL. page.url() after load equals the injected attacker domain.
- 🟡 **JavaScript Framework Debug / Development Mode Enabled in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/occasional/passive
  - discovery: After page load, execute page.evaluate to check for __REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__, window.__VUE__, window.ng (Angular DevMode), and the size/contents of window.__NEXT_DATA__. Inspect inline <script id='__NEXT_DATA__'> JSON for env vars, API keys, or server-side props. Check if React is running in development mode via __REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__.renderers.
  - signal: page.evaluate returns truthy for __REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__ on a production domain. window.__NEXT_DATA__ contains keys like env, serverSideProps, or buildId with non-production values. Angular ng.probe() is callable on the window. Vue devtools warning banner visible in page text. Credential patterns match inside the __NEXT_DATA__ JSON blob.
- 🟡 **CORS Wildcard on Sensitive / Authenticated API Endpoint** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Send a GET request to each discovered API endpoint with a cross-origin Origin header. If the response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * AND the response body contains patterns matching PII, tokens, user data, or structured JSON with field names like email, userId, token — flag it. Distinguish from static asset wildcards (images, fonts) by checking Content-Type and response body structure.
  - signal: Response header Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on an endpoint whose Content-Type is application/json AND whose body matches at least one credential or PII detector pattern (email address, userId, JWT, access_token field). Differs from the credentialed CORS finding — credentials header not required, but data sensitivity is the signal.
- 🟡 **Missing Subresource Integrity (SRI) on Third-Party Scripts** — _A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Crawl each rendered page and parse all <script src=...> and <link rel=stylesheet href=...> tags whose src/href points to a domain other than the first-party origin. Check whether each external tag includes a valid integrity= hash attribute. Also flag <script src=> pointing to third-party domains served without HTTPS.
  - signal: HTML DOM contains at least one <script src=https://cdn.example.com/...> or <link href=https://...> where the src/href host does not match the page origin AND the tag lacks an integrity= attribute. Pure passive DOM observation — no active probe needed.

## REST / GraphQL API  (15)

- 🟠 **Excessive Data Exposure in API Responses** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Crawl authenticated endpoints and compare the full JSON response body against what the UI renders. Flag any response field containing patterns matching password, hash, secret, ssn, dob, internal_id, is_admin, role, token, credit_card. Diff responses for two users of different privilege levels to identify fields visible to both that should be role-gated.
  - signal: Response JSON contains field names matching a sensitive-keyword regex (e.g., `password`, `hash`, `secret`, `token`, `ssn`, `dob`, `is_admin`) not present in the rendered DOM; or low-privilege user response contains fields identical to high-privilege user response for the same resource.
- 🔴 **Broken Object Property Level Authorization (Mass Assignment / BOPLA)** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: For every PATCH/PUT endpoint discovered during crawl, replay the request with injected extra properties (role, is_admin, plan, credits, verified, locked) appended to the JSON body. Then GET the same resource and check whether the injected property was accepted and reflected in the response.
  - signal: After sending a PATCH/PUT body containing injected properties, a subsequent GET response reflects the injected field value (e.g., `is_admin: true`, `role: admin`, `balance: 99999`). HTTP 200 on the write plus changed value on the read confirms acceptance.
- 🔴 **Broken Object Level Authorization (IDOR) via API Resource Enumeration** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: After authenticating as User A, collect all resource IDs seen in crawled responses. As User B (different account), request those same IDs. Compare HTTP status and response body. Also fuzz ID parameters by incrementing/decrementing numeric IDs and substituting known-foreign UUIDs. A 200 with another user's data is a confirmed finding.
  - signal: User B receives HTTP 200 with a response body whose owner/user_id field differs from User B's own ID; or a resource fetched by User B matches data created exclusively under User A's session. Any non-403/404 response to a foreign-ID request is flagged.
- 🟠 **Missing Rate Limiting on Authentication / Credential Endpoints** — _A04:2021 Insecure Design_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send 50-100 rapid POST requests to the login/auth endpoint with varying passwords for a known username. Observe response codes, headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After), and body messages. Absence of 429 responses, account lockout messages, or any rate-limit header after sustained bursting confirms the gap.
  - signal: After N rapid sequential requests (e.g., 50) to `/api/auth/login` or `/api/v1/password-reset`, no HTTP 429 is observed, no `Retry-After` or `X-RateLimit-Remaining` header appears, and responses remain structurally identical (no lockout body). Timing between responses stays consistently low (<200ms).
- 🟡 **Missing Rate Limiting on Business-Logic API Endpoints** — _A04:2021 Insecure Design_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Identify high-cost endpoints from crawled traffic (search, export, bulk-fetch). Send 100 rapid identical authenticated requests and measure: response codes, any rate-limit headers, server response time degradation, and whether the operation succeeds on all 100 requests. No 429 and consistent success across all requests is the finding.
  - signal: 100 rapid authenticated GET/POST requests to a search or export endpoint all return HTTP 200 with no `X-RateLimit-*` headers and no response-time increase above 2x baseline. Cumulative data volume retrievable in under 10 seconds exceeds what a human interaction would produce.
- 🟡 **User Enumeration via API Response Differences** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Submit registration or password-reset requests with a known-existing email and a known-nonexistent email (or random UUID). Compare HTTP status codes, response body strings (e.g., 'email not found' vs 'reset link sent'), and response times. Any observable difference (textual, status, or timing >100ms) confirms enumeration.
  - signal: Two requests to `/api/auth/forgot-password`: one with a seeded valid email, one with a random nonexistent email. Observable diff in status code (200 vs 404), body text ('user not found' vs 'check your email'), or response time delta >150ms. Automated diff of trimmed response bodies flags the divergence.
- 🟡 **Resource/Object Enumeration via Predictable API Identifiers** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Extract all numeric and short-slug IDs from crawled responses. Probe a range of adjacent IDs (current-50 to current+50) as an unauthenticated or low-privilege user. Compare HTTP 200/403/404 distribution. A 403 confirms existence while 404 denies it — any 403 for a foreign-user resource is an enumeration signal.
  - signal: Sequential ID probing yields mixed 200/403 vs uniform 404 responses. A 403 on `/api/orders/1042` while the authenticated user only owns order 2991 indicates the object exists and the server leaks existence via status code rather than returning a consistent 404 for all unauthorized resources.
- 🟠 **Improper API Inventory — Unretired Legacy API Versions** — _A09:2021 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: After discovering the current API version prefix from crawled traffic (e.g., `/api/v3/`), probe earlier version prefixes (`/api/v1/`, `/api/v2/`, `/api/beta/`, `/api/legacy/`, `/v1/`) on every endpoint discovered. Compare authentication requirements, response schemas, and rate-limit behavior between versions.
  - signal: An endpoint at `/api/v1/users` returns HTTP 200 with a full user list while the same path at `/api/v3/users` returns 403 for the same unauthenticated or low-privilege request. Or the v1 path accepts requests without a required Authorization header that v3 enforces. Version-path fuzzing confirms reachability.
- 🟡 **Verbose API Error Responses Leaking Stack Traces / Internal Details** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Submit malformed inputs to all discovered endpoints: invalid types (string where integer expected), boundary values (negative IDs, extremely long strings, null bytes), malformed JSON bodies, and unknown fields. Inspect every non-2xx response body for stack trace markers, file path strings, SQL fragments, or framework/library name strings.
  - signal: Response body contains any of: multi-line stack trace with file paths (`at /app/src/...`), SQL error text (`PG::UndefinedColumn`, `SQLite3::Exception`, `ORA-00942`), internal hostnames or IP addresses, ORM model class names, or framework-specific debug keys (`debug`, `traceback`, `exception`, `stack`). Regex scan on all error responses.
- 🟡 **GraphQL Introspection Enabled in Production** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Send a POST request to the GraphQL endpoint with the standard introspection query body (`{__schema{types{name fields{name type{name}}}}}` and `{__typename}`). A successful response with schema data confirms introspection is enabled. Also probe common GraphQL paths: `/graphql`, `/api/graphql`, `/query`, `/gql`.
  - signal: POST to `/graphql` with `{"query":"{__schema{queryType{name}}}"}` returns HTTP 200 with a JSON body containing `__schema` data rather than an error or disabled-introspection message. Response body size is typically >1KB and contains type definitions.
- ⚪ **GraphQL Field Suggestion / Did-You-Mean Enumeration** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  low/common/active
  - discovery: With introspection disabled, send GraphQL queries containing deliberately misspelled field and type names (e.g., `{usr{id}}`, `{userz{email}}`). Parse error messages from the response for suggestion strings. Iterate systematically with common word variations to walk the schema tree via suggestions.
  - signal: GraphQL error response body contains a string matching `Did you mean` or `Suggestions:` followed by a valid field name. This occurs even without introspection enabled. The presence of any suggestion string in an error response on a production endpoint is the flag.
- 🔴 **Broken Function Level Authorization — Admin API Endpoint Accessible to Regular Users** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the application as an admin user and catalog all API endpoints. Replay those same requests using a non-admin user's session token. Compare response status codes and body content. Probe common admin path patterns (`/api/admin/`, `/api/internal/`, `/api/management/`, `/api/v1/admin`) using the non-admin session.
  - signal: A request to `/api/admin/users` or `/api/internal/config` using a regular-user JWT or session cookie returns HTTP 200 with data, rather than 401 or 403. Auth-diff comparison shows admin-session response and regular-user-session response are identical (same status, same structure, same data volume).
- 🟠 **Shadow / Undocumented API Endpoints Accessible Without Authentication** — _A09:2021 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Fuzz common unauthenticated paths against the API base: `/api/health`, `/api/debug`, `/api/status`, `/api/metrics`, `/api/ping`, `/api/version`, `/api/env`, `/api/config`, `/api/test`, `/api/swagger`, `/api/openapi.json`, `/api/docs`. Send requests with no Authorization header and flag any 200 response that returns structured data beyond a simple status string.
  - signal: GET request to `/api/debug`, `/api/env`, or `/api/internal/metrics` without any Authorization header returns HTTP 200 with a JSON body containing application internals, dependency versions, environment variable names, or database connection strings. Any non-401/403 response to a credential-free request on a non-public path.
- 🔴 **Missing Authentication on State-Mutating API Endpoints** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  critical/occasional/active
  - discovery: For every mutating endpoint captured during authenticated crawl, replay the request stripped of all authentication material (remove Authorization header, session cookie, and X-API-Key). Compare the response: a 200 or 201 without credentials confirms the finding. Also test with an expired or invalidated token.
  - signal: POST to `/api/orders` or DELETE to `/api/users/42` with the Authorization header entirely absent (or replaced with an expired JWT) returns HTTP 200, 201, or 204 rather than 401. Response body confirms the mutation was accepted (e.g., `id` in creation response, empty body on delete with 204).
- 🟠 **GraphQL Query Batching / Alias Amplification Without Rate Limiting** — _A04:2021 Insecure Design_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send a POST to the GraphQL endpoint with a JSON array body containing 50 identical login mutation operations (batching) or a single query with 50 aliases for the same field. Measure response time vs a single-operation baseline. Also attempt 50 batched login mutations with different passwords to test brute-force potential per HTTP request.
  - signal: A POST body containing a JSON array of 50 operations returns HTTP 200 with an array of 50 results, response time under 5x the single-request baseline (indicating parallel execution), and no 429 or complexity-limit error. For aliased queries, a single request returns 50 separate resolver results under the alias keys.

## Ethical-hacker recon  (15)

- 🔴 **Exposed .git Directory** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Probe /.git/HEAD with a GET request. A 200 response containing the string 'ref: refs/heads/' is a definitive hit. Follow up with /.git/config to extract remote URLs and branch names.
  - signal: HTTP 200 on /.git/HEAD with body matching 'ref: refs/heads/'. HTTP 200 on /.git/config with '[core]' or '[remote' in body. Any non-404/403 status on /.git/COMMIT_EDITMSG.
- 🔴 **Exposed .env / Environment File** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Request /.env, /.env.local, /.env.production, /.env.backup, /.env.example in sequence. Check for KEY=VALUE patterns in the response body.
  - signal: HTTP 200 with response body containing lines matching the pattern VARIABLE=value, APP_KEY=, DB_PASSWORD=, SECRET_KEY=, or DATABASE_URL=.
- 🟡 **Directory Listing Enabled** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Crawl the app and follow links to paths ending without a filename extension. Probe common upload/static directories (/uploads/, /backup/, /files/, /static/, /assets/). Check if the response contains an HTML index listing.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response body containing 'Index of /' or '<title>Directory listing' or an Apache/Nginx autoindex block with <a href> file entries. Also detectable via IIS server 'Directory Listing' title.
- 🟡 **Verbose Error / Stack Trace Disclosure** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Submit malformed input to every form and URL parameter: empty required fields, extremely long strings, type-mismatches (string where integer expected), special characters (', ", <, ;, null bytes). Observe whether the error response leaks framework internals.
  - signal: Response body contains 'Traceback (most recent call last)', 'at com.', 'java.lang.NullPointerException', 'RuntimeError', 'ActionView::Template::Error', 'Whoops\Exception', 'Exception in thread', or full file-system paths like '/var/www/', '/home/deploy/'.
- 🟡 **Missing / Weak Security Response Headers** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Fetch the app root and any authenticated page with a plain GET. Inspect all response headers for presence and correct values of the standard defensive set.
  - signal: Absence of Content-Security-Policy header, or X-Frame-Options missing/set to ALLOWALL, or X-Content-Type-Options not set to 'nosniff', or Strict-Transport-Security absent on HTTPS responses, or Server header exposing exact version string (e.g., 'Apache/2.4.51').
- 🟠 **Session Cookie Missing Secure / HttpOnly / SameSite Flags** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/passive
  - discovery: Perform a login flow and capture Set-Cookie headers on the authentication response. Parse flag presence for every cookie that appears to carry a session token or authentication value.
  - signal: Set-Cookie header for a session/auth cookie (name matches session, token, auth, _session, JSESSIONID, PHPSESSID, connect.sid, etc.) where the header string does not contain 'HttpOnly', does not contain 'Secure', or contains 'SameSite=None' without 'Secure'.
- ⚪ **Server / Framework Version Disclosure via Response Headers** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  low/ubiquitous/passive
  - discovery: Fetch any page (even a 404) and inspect Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version, X-Runtime, X-Generator, and Via headers. Version strings confirm the technology fingerprint.
  - signal: X-Powered-By header present with value like 'PHP/8.1.3', 'Express', 'ASP.NET'; Server header containing version like 'Apache/2.4.51 (Ubuntu)' or 'nginx/1.18.0'; X-AspNet-Version header present; X-Generator set to 'WordPress 6.x'.
- 🟠 **Debug / Admin Endpoint Publicly Accessible** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Enumerate a wordlist of common debug/admin paths: /admin, /admin/, /django-admin/, /actuator, /actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump, /_profiler, /telescope, /horizon, /rails/info, /phpMyAdmin/, /adminer.php, /wp-admin/, /_ah/admin, /debug/pprof. Record HTTP status codes and response bodies.
  - signal: HTTP 200 (or 302 redirect to a login form) for any probed admin/debug path. For Spring Actuator specifically, a 200 on /actuator/env with JSON body containing 'propertySources'. For Django admin, a login form with 'Django administration' in the title. For /actuator/heapdump, a binary download response.
- 🔴 **Default / Weak Credentials on Admin Interfaces** — _A07:2021 Identification and Authentication Failures_  ·  critical/common/active
  - discovery: Locate any admin login form (discovered via path enumeration or crawl). Submit a short list of known default credential pairs: admin/admin, admin/password, admin/123456, root/root, user/user. For WordPress, also try admin/<domain-name>. Observe whether the authentication succeeds.
  - signal: HTTP 302 redirect to a dashboard URL (e.g., /wp-admin/index.php, /dashboard, /home) after POST to login endpoint, or HTTP 200 with a page title containing 'Dashboard', 'Welcome', or the application name without an error message. Absence of CAPTCHA or lockout on first attempt also signals weak auth hygiene.
- 🔴 **Hardcoded Secrets / API Keys in Served JavaScript** — _A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures_  ·  critical/common/passive
  - discovery: Crawl the app and collect all <script src> URLs. Fetch each JS file (including webpack chunks named like main.chunk.js, vendor.js, runtime~main.js). Scan the text content for high-entropy strings and patterns matching common API key formats (AWS, Stripe, Twilio, Google, SendGrid, etc.).
  - signal: JS response body containing patterns like AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16} (AWS access key), sk_live_[0-9a-zA-Z]{24,} (Stripe secret), AIza[0-9A-Za-z_-]{35} (Google API), SG\.[a-zA-Z0-9]{22}\.[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{43} (SendGrid), or generic high-entropy assignments (apiKey: 'XXXX', secret = 'XXXX').
- 🟠 **Hidden API Endpoints Leaked in JavaScript Bundles** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Fetch all JavaScript files delivered to unauthenticated and authenticated sessions. Extract all string literals matching URL path patterns (/api/, /v1/, /internal/, /admin/, /graphql, /rpc/). Diff the two sets to find endpoints only visible post-auth. Probe extracted paths to determine which respond without authentication.
  - signal: String literals in JS matching path patterns /api/admin/, /internal/, /v[0-9]/, /_debug/, /rpc/ that return HTTP 200 or 401 (rather than 404) when probed. A 200 response to a path found only in JS (not in crawled HTML links) confirms a hidden-but-live endpoint.
- 🟠 **Overly Permissive CORS Policy** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  high/common/active
  - discovery: Send requests to API endpoints with crafted Origin headers: a random domain (Origin: https://evil.com), the target with a suffix (Origin: https://target.com.evil.com), and null (Origin: null). Observe if Access-Control-Allow-Origin mirrors the submitted value and whether Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is 'true' simultaneously.
  - signal: Response header Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com (mirroring the injected origin) combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. OR Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on an endpoint that also sets a session cookie. OR Access-Control-Allow-Origin: null allowing null-origin requests.
- 🟡 **JavaScript Source Map Files Publicly Accessible** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  medium/common/active
  - discovery: Fetch each JavaScript bundle and scan the last lines for //# sourceMappingURL=. Construct the full URL for the referenced .map file and attempt to fetch it. Parse the sources and sourcesContent fields of the JSON to enumerate original file paths.
  - signal: HTTP 200 response to a .js.map URL with a JSON body containing a 'sources' array of original file paths (e.g., src/config/api.js, src/utils/auth.js) and optionally a 'sourcesContent' array with the raw source code. Presence of sourcesContent confirms full source reconstruction is possible.
- ⚪ **Path / Endpoint Disclosure via robots.txt and sitemap.xml** — _A01:2021 Broken Access Control_  ·  low/ubiquitous/active
  - discovery: Fetch /robots.txt and /sitemap.xml (and /sitemap_index.xml) as the very first step. Extract all Disallow and Allow paths from robots.txt and all <loc> values from sitemaps. Probe each discovered path to determine accessibility without authentication.
  - signal: robots.txt Disallow lines containing /admin, /internal, /api/, /backup, /staging, /private, or /debug. sitemap.xml <loc> entries pointing to pages not linked from public navigation. Any Disallow path that responds with 200 rather than 401/403 when probed.
- 🟠 **Dangling DNS / Subdomain Takeover** — _A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration_  ·  high/occasional/active
  - discovery: Enumerate subdomains via certificate transparency (crt.sh), DNS brute-force, and permutation scanning. For each live CNAME, resolve the final target and send an HTTP request. Check for service-specific 'unclaimed' fingerprints in the response body.
  - signal: HTTP response body matching known dangling-service fingerprints: 'There is no app here yet' (Heroku), 'NoSuchBucket' (AWS S3), '404 — There is nothing here' (GitHub Pages), 'Repository not found' (Bitbucket), 'The site you were looking for couldn\'t be found' (Netlify), or NXDOMAIN on a CNAME chain target while the apex subdomain still resolves.
